If staff only record billed invoices, the reports may omit real obligations and understate expenses. Accrual discipline is especially important in NGOs where services are received continuously, such as rent, utilities, consulting, payroll, and field support. Correct classification helps management understand what is already owed versus what is only expected to be billed later.
Send to the team:
- “Please distinguish billed obligations from unbilled accruals before closing the period.”
Key point:
- Payables are typically billed; accruals are incurred but not yet billed.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
8. Q: What is the difference between a receivable and a cash receipt?
A: A receivable is the amount the NGO expects to collect but has not yet received. A cash receipt is the actual movement of cash into the NGO’s bank account or cash box, which settles all or part of that receivable. The distinction is important because recording cash alone does not explain what was earned, billed, or still outstanding. If the organization does not match cash receipts to the related receivable, it may leave items unapplied, duplicate income, or lose track of which donor or customer paid what. Proper matching also helps with donor reporting and bank reconciliation. A disciplined receipt process makes it easier to see which balances remain open and which have been collected.
Send to the team:
- “Please match each cash receipt to the related receivable and clear the open item.”
Key point:
- Receivable means owed; cash receipt means collected.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
9. Q: How should an NGO process supplier invoices?
A: The organization should process supplier invoices through a controlled sequence rather than treating them as simple paperwork. First, the invoice should be received and logged so it cannot be lost or paid twice. Next, finance should check completeness, vendor identity, math accuracy, tax details if relevant, and whether the charge matches the approved purchase or service arrangement. Then the invoice should be coded to the correct account, project, cost center, or grant, reviewed by the appropriate budget holder, and approved according to authority limits. Only after those checks should it be posted and scheduled for payment. Any exception, mismatch, or missing support should be resolved before cash is released whenever possible.
Send to the team:
- “Please process supplier invoices only after document, coding, and approval checks are complete.”
Key point:
- Invoice processing should follow a controlled and documented sequence.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
10. Q: What documents should support an accounts payable entry?
A: A strong accounts payable entry should be supported by the supplier invoice and any documents that explain why the NGO owes the money. Depending on the transaction, that support may include a purchase order, receiving report, contract, quotation, service completion evidence, approval record, and vendor details. For travel, workshops, reimbursements, or special payments, the support may look different, but the principle is the same: the entry should be traceable to real activity and an authorized decision. Good support makes it easier to defend the balance during audit, to identify duplicates, and to confirm that the cost belongs to the right grant or program. Weak support creates uncertainty and increases the risk of misposting. A well-filed AP package also helps staff who need to answer donor or management questions later.
Send to the team:
- “Please file the invoice with the approval, receiving evidence, and any related contract or purchase support.”
Key point:
- AP entries should be backed by source documents that explain the liability.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
11. Q: What is the AP workflow in a nonprofit?
A: The accounts payable workflow usually starts when an invoice, claim, or payment request is received and logged. Finance then checks the support, verifies the coding, confirms the approver, and reviews whether the expense is valid for the budget or grant. After review, the item is posted to the ledger, prepared for payment in a batch or payment run, and then released according to approval rules. After payment, the bank transaction must be reconciled back to the original invoice so the open item is cleared. This workflow may look simple, but it is one of the main control systems in the organization. If any step is skipped, the NGO risks duplicate payments, missing liabilities, and weak reporting quality.
Send to the team:
- “Please follow one AP workflow for all suppliers and keep evidence at each step.”
Key point:
- AP should move through documented control steps from receipt to reconciliation.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
12. Q: Who should review an invoice before payment?
A: Review should normally happen at two levels: a finance review and a business or budget review. The finance reviewer checks arithmetic, coding, supporting documents, vendor details, and whether the item is properly entered in the records. The budget holder or program manager confirms that the goods or services were needed, received, and consistent with the approved activity or project. In larger NGOs, these roles may be split more formally, while in smaller organizations one person may perform more than one review step but should not collapse all approval authority into a single unchecked action. The point is not bureaucracy; it is making sure the NGO does not pay for a cost that was never received, never approved, or coded to the wrong place.
Send to the team:
- “Please have finance and the budget owner review each invoice before payment.”
Key point:
- Review should combine finance checks and budget-owner validation.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
13. Q: Who should approve a payment in an NGO?
A: A payment should be approved by a person who has authority under the NGO’s delegation matrix and who is independent enough to make a proper decision. That may be a manager, finance head, executive director, or another designated signatory, depending on the amount, purpose, and risk level. The approval should not be informal or assumed just because someone is senior. It should be visible in the file, linked to the payment batch or voucher, and consistent with the approved authority limits. This matters because authority is one of the main internal controls protecting the organization from unauthorized commitments and unsupported disbursements. In a donor-funded setting, it also helps show that spending followed the NGO’s own rules and the donor’s expectations.
Send to the team:
- “Please use the delegated authority matrix for every payment approval.”
Key point:
- Payment approval must follow written authority limits.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
14. Q: Why should invoice approval be separate from payment release?
A: Approval and payment release should be separated so no single person can approve a cost and also move the cash without a second layer of oversight. That separation reduces the risk of fraud, duplicate payment, accidental payment of the wrong amount, and self-approval. It also improves the quality of decisions because the reviewer can focus on whether the expense is valid, while the payment processor focuses on whether the bank instructions are correct. In practical terms, this means one person may confirm that a supplier is owed and another person or system may release the actual money. For NGOs with limited staff, the separation can be achieved through shared review, signatory control, or system-based controls rather than full staffing duplication.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate approval of invoices from release of payments wherever staffing allows.”
Key point:
- Separation of duties strengthens control over cash and liability decisions.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
15. Q: How should an NGO record a supplier invoice that is incomplete?
A: An incomplete invoice should not be treated as fully ready for posting unless the missing information is minor and the organization has a documented rule for handling it. Finance should identify what is missing, request a corrected invoice or supporting evidence, and decide whether the item belongs in suspense, pending review, or accrual status. If the service was clearly received but the invoice is incomplete, the NGO may still need to recognize the obligation using an accrual approach and then clear it when the proper invoice arrives. The key is not to pretend the problem does not exist. A poor response to incomplete invoices creates disputes later and can cause errors in grant coding, vendor balances, and month-end reporting. The file should always show how the issue was resolved.
Send to the team:
- “Please do not finalize incomplete invoices until the missing support is explained or corrected.”
Key point:
- Incomplete invoices need follow-up, control judgment, or accrual treatment.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
16. Q: What is the role of three-way matching in transaction processing?
A: Three-way matching is the comparison of the purchase order, receiving evidence, and supplier invoice before payment is approved. It gives the NGO a practical way to confirm that what was ordered is what was received, and that the invoice agrees with both. This control is especially useful where goods are purchased frequently, quantities matter, or the organization wants to prevent overbilling and duplicate billing. In an NGO environment, three-way matching also supports donor accountability because it demonstrates that spending followed a traceable chain from request to delivery to billing. It does not eliminate the need for judgment, but it is one of the strongest routine checks available for procurement-linked payments. When used properly, it reduces both error and fraud risk.
Send to the team:
- “Please match the PO, receiving report, and invoice before payment is authorized.”
Key point:
- Three-way matching confirms order, receipt, and billing consistency.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
17. Q: What is three-way matching between purchase order, receiving, and invoice?
A: It is a control process that compares three separate records: the original purchase order, the evidence that the goods or services were received, and the supplier’s invoice. The aim is to confirm that the quantity, price, vendor, and timing are all consistent before cash is released. If the invoice shows more items than were received, a different unit price, or an item that was never ordered, the mismatch should be flagged and resolved. This step protects the NGO from paying for materials not delivered or for services not provided as agreed. It also makes it easier to assign responsibility when something goes wrong because the review trail is visible. In many NGOs, this is one of the easiest controls to standardize across programs and field locations.
Send to the team:
- “Please compare the order, receipt, and invoice line by line before payment.”
Key point:
- Three-way matching tests consistency across three records.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
18. Q: Why is three-way matching important for control?
A: Three-way matching prevents the organization from paying for items it did not order, did not receive, or did not agree to at the stated price. It also catches duplicate invoices, short deliveries, and clerical errors before money leaves the bank. For a nonprofit, this matters because every unnecessary payment weakens resources that should have supported mission work. The control also gives auditors and donors evidence that the NGO has a structured process rather than relying on memory or informal conversations. If the process is used consistently, staff begin to expect that every payment must be explained by supporting records. That cultural discipline is often as valuable as the mechanical check itself.
Send to the team:
- “Please require a three-way match before approving payment.”
Key point:
- Three-way matching is a core anti-error and anti-fraud control.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
19. Q: How should an NGO handle invoices without a purchase order?
A: Invoices without a purchase order should be treated as exceptions, not as normal routine items. The NGO should determine whether the spend was urgent, whether the PO process was not required, or whether the control was bypassed incorrectly. If the organization allows non-PO spending in some cases, those cases should be defined in policy and approved retrospectively with written explanation. If the invoice represents a pattern of bypassing controls, management should investigate because repeated exceptions often signal weak procurement discipline or poor communication between programs and finance. A well-run NGO does not simply ignore non-PO invoices; it documents them, reviews them, and learns from them. Over time, this improves compliance and reduces disputes.
Send to the team:
- “Please flag non-PO invoices for exception review and do not treat them as routine.”
Key point:
- Non-PO invoices need exception control and documented justification.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
20. Q: What is a payment batch?
A: A payment batch is a group of approved payments prepared together for bank transfer, cheque issuance, or another payment channel. The batch groups items so finance can review the total amount, confirm the payees, and release funds in a more controlled and efficient way. It is not just a convenience tool; it is also a control point because batch totals and item lists can be checked before release. In an NGO, batching helps with busy payment cycles such as payroll, vendor settlements, field reimbursements, and grant-related payments. It also supports smoother bank reconciliation because the bank movement can be tied back to a defined list of authorized items. A well-managed batch process reduces manual work while keeping oversight visible.
Send to the team:
- “Please group approved payments into controlled batches and review the totals before release.”
Key point:
- A batch is a controlled group of payments prepared for release.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
21. Q: Why are payment batches used in NGOs?
A: Payment batches are used because they make review, approval, and processing more efficient while still allowing control over what is being paid. Instead of handling many individual transactions one by one at the point of release, finance can inspect the batch as a group and still confirm each line item. This is helpful in NGOs where administrative teams are small and payment volumes may be spread across many projects or field sites. Batches also support stronger control over cash planning because management can see the total cash impact before funds leave the bank. When paired with proper authorization and reconciliations, batching helps the organization work faster without weakening accountability. It also makes audit follow-up easier because the batch file becomes a single control package.
Send to the team:
- “Please review batch totals and item lists before any payment run is released.”
Key point:
- Batches improve efficiency and control at the same time.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
22. Q: What should be checked before a payment batch is released?
A: Before release, the batch should be checked for correct approvals, matching support, valid payee details, duplicate entries, correct amounts, and budget availability. The batch total should be compared against the approved authorization, and the bank instructions should be verified carefully because errors at that stage can be difficult to reverse. If the batch contains items for different programs or grants, the coding should also be checked so reporting remains accurate. This review is a practical gate between finance records and cash movement. Once money is sent, the organization often has less leverage to correct mistakes quickly. A disciplined batch review therefore protects both cash and the quality of the ledger.
Send to the team:
- “Please verify every batch item, the batch total, and the bank details before release.”
Key point:
- Batch release needs both line-item checks and total checks.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
23. Q: What is a payment voucher?
A: A payment voucher is an internal control document that summarizes the payment to be made and explains why it is valid. It usually shows the payee, amount, purpose, account coding, support documents, approval evidence, and payment method. In many NGOs, the voucher acts as the bridge between the invoice file and the payment run. It is useful because it creates a single package for review rather than forcing reviewers to chase separate papers across different files. A good voucher reduces confusion, speeds approval, and makes audit testing easier. It also supports accountability by showing who reviewed the payment and on what basis the cash was released.
Send to the team:
- “Please complete the payment voucher before any payment is released.”
Key point:
- A voucher is the internal payment authorization record.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
24. Q: What information should be included in a payment voucher?
A: A complete voucher should identify the payee, the amount, the purpose of the payment, the date or period covered, the account or project code, and the supporting documents attached to the request. It should also show who prepared the voucher, who reviewed it, who approved it, and what payment method will be used. If the item relates to a grant, donor, or restricted fund, that connection should be visible so the payment can be traced correctly in reporting. Good voucher design is less about formality and more about making sure someone can understand the payment months later without needing verbal explanations. The voucher should be complete enough that another staff member or auditor could follow the trail without guessing. That is what makes it useful as a control record.
Send to the team:
- “Please make the voucher complete before routing it for approval.”
Key point:
- Vouchers should show who, what, why, how much, and under which authority.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
25. Q: What is the difference between a supplier advance and a supplier payable?
A: A supplier advance is money paid before the goods or services have been received. A supplier payable is the opposite timing: the goods or services have already been received, and the NGO now owes the money. This difference matters because advances usually sit as prepayments or advances until they are cleared, while payables sit as liabilities until settlement. Mixing them up can distort both the balance sheet and the expense recognition pattern. In nonprofit finance, this distinction is especially important when an NGO pays workshops, transport, medical supplies, or field services in advance. If the advance is not tracked carefully, the organization may lose visibility over whether the delivery actually happened.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate advance payments from trade payables in the ledger and follow up on liquidation.”
Key point:
- An advance is prepaid; a payable is owed after receipt.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
26. Q: How should supplier advances be recorded?
A: Supplier advances should normally be recorded as an asset or prepayment until the related goods or services are received or the advance is otherwise properly liquidated. Recording the amount as expense too early can overstate costs and hide unresolved delivery issues. The advance account should show the vendor, project, date, purpose, and expected settlement evidence so finance can monitor whether the balance is aging beyond a reasonable period. This is particularly important in NGOs that pay deposits to vendors, trainers, community mobilizers, or logistics providers before services are fully delivered. A disciplined advance process helps management see where cash has gone and whether the organization has received value in return. It also gives audit reviewers a clean path for tracing the funds.
Send to the team:
- “Please record supplier advances in a separate advance account until they are cleared by support.”
Key point:
- Advances are not final expenses until the related delivery is verified.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
27. Q: How should supplier advances be cleared?
A: Supplier advances should be cleared when the related invoice, delivery evidence, service completion record, or other agreed support shows that the vendor has performed. The clearing entry should match the original advance to the final settlement so the balance does not stay open after the obligation has been satisfied. If the vendor has not yet performed, the advance should remain open and should be monitored rather than silently reclassified away. A good clearing process is important because it tells management whether the organization is still waiting on delivery or whether the transaction has been completed. It also helps prevent the same payment from being counted twice, once as an advance and again as a final expense. Regular review of open advances keeps the ledger clean and honest.
Send to the team:
- “Please liquidate supplier advances only against the correct supporting evidence.”
Key point:
- Advances should be matched and cleared promptly when delivery is confirmed.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
28. Q: What is the risk of leaving supplier advances uncleared?
A: Uncleared advances can hide missing deliverables, weak vendor follow-up, or poor recordkeeping. They may also make the balance sheet look stronger than it really is by showing an asset that no longer reflects realistic recoverability or service delivery status. In a nonprofit setting, long-outstanding advances can also indicate fraud risk, especially if funds were transferred to a vendor or staff member without adequate accountability. Even when there is no fraud, uncleared balances create confusion at month-end and make it harder to determine which projects have actually used their cash. The longer the item stays open, the harder it becomes to explain. Regular review turns the advance account from a dumping ground into a proper control schedule.
Send to the team:
- “Please review uncleared advances every month and escalate old items.”
Key point:
- Uncleared advances can conceal delivery, accounting, and control problems.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
29. Q: What is an aged payables report?
A: An aged payables report lists unpaid supplier balances by how long they have been outstanding. Common buckets are current, 30 days, 60 days, 90 days, and older, although the exact format can be adjusted to the organization’s needs. The purpose is to help finance see which liabilities are recent and which are becoming overdue or stuck. In an NGO, this is valuable because long-outstanding payables may signal missing documents, disputed charges, or cash pressure. Aged payables also support treasury planning because they show what is likely to need payment soon. When reviewed regularly, the report becomes a practical management tool rather than a passive schedule.
Send to the team:
- “Please prepare the payables aging every month and review old items with the budget holders.”
Key point:
- Aging shows how old unpaid liabilities are and where attention is needed.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
30. Q: Why should payables aging be reviewed regularly?
A: Regular review of payables aging helps the NGO spot overdue bills, unresolved disputes, duplicate liabilities, and items that should be reversed or followed up. It also helps treasury staff understand how much cash will be needed soon, which is important when donor receipts are uneven or restricted. Reviewing the aging is also a practical control against errors because old or unusual balances often reveal process problems. If a payable has remained open too long, finance should ask why it has not been paid, matched, cleared, or disputed. This kind of monthly review improves the quality of the books and keeps liability reporting trustworthy. It also gives management a useful discussion point with procurement and program teams.
Send to the team:
- “Please review aged payables and investigate items that remain open beyond normal settlement time.”
Key point:
- Aging review supports cash planning, error detection, and cleanup.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
31. Q: What causes old outstanding payables in an NGO?
A: Old outstanding payables can arise from missing support, delayed approvals, disputes over the goods or services, duplicate entries that were never cleared, or accruals that were recorded but not reversed properly. Sometimes the real problem is operational rather than accounting: procurement may not have confirmed receipt, or a program team may not have informed finance that a service had been completed. In other cases, the balance may reflect a vendor that was paid elsewhere or a credit note that was not linked to the original invoice. These are not harmless errors, because they can distort liabilities and make the organization appear more indebted than it is. Old payables should always be investigated rather than rolled forward mechanically. The goal is to convert stale balances into explained and supportable items or to remove them properly.
Send to the team:
- “Please investigate every old payable before carrying it forward again.”
Key point:
- Old payables often signal weak follow-up, missing documents, or stale accruals.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
32. Q: How should disputed supplier invoices be handled?
A: Disputed supplier invoices should be placed under formal review and not treated as routine payables until the issue is resolved. The dispute file should explain what is being challenged, who raised the issue, whether the problem is quantity, price, quality, timing, or authorization, and what communication has already taken place with the supplier. If the NGO owes only part of the invoice, a partial settlement may be appropriate, but the decision should be documented clearly. A dispute should never disappear simply because it is inconvenient to track. In many NGOs, disputed invoices reveal procurement breakdowns or field-level misunderstandings that can be corrected only if the issue is visible. Tracking them separately helps management protect both cash and relationships with vendors.
Send to the team:
- “Please log disputed invoices separately and follow each one through resolution.”
Key point:
- Disputed invoices need separate tracking and documented action.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
33. Q: How should credit notes from suppliers be recorded?
A: Credit notes should be linked to the original invoice or payment so the accounting impact is clear. Depending on the circumstances, they may reduce an expense, reduce a payable, reduce an asset value, or create a receivable from the supplier if cash was already paid. The important point is that the credit note should not be treated as isolated paper; it should close or adjust the original transaction. If the organization ignores supplier credits, the ledger may show an overstated cost or an overstated liability. In a nonprofit, that can affect donor reporting, budget variance analysis, and program cost measurement. Good processing of credit notes also shows that finance is following through on recoveries and adjustments rather than leaving value unclaimed.
Send to the team:
- “Please match every credit note to the original invoice or payment and update the ledger accordingly.”
Key point:
- Supplier credits reduce or reverse the original charge.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
34. Q: What is the difference between goods received and invoice received?
A: Goods received means the organization has taken delivery of the item or the service has been completed. Invoice received means the supplier’s bill has arrived. These are separate events and they often happen on different dates, which is why finance should track both. If the NGO has received the goods but not the invoice, it may need an accrual or received-not-billed adjustment. If the invoice arrives before delivery, that may indicate a prepaid arrangement or a control issue that needs review. Accurate separation of the two dates improves cut-off accuracy and makes it easier to explain month-end balances. It also helps program staff and finance staff speak the same language when they review open items.
Send to the team:
- “Please record receiving and invoicing as separate events in the transaction file.”
Key point:
- Receipt and invoicing do not always happen at the same time, and both matter.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
35. Q: How should received but unbilled items be treated?
A: Received but unbilled items should generally be recognized through an accrual or similar liability estimate when the NGO has already consumed the goods or services but has not yet received the invoice. This is necessary so expenses are recorded in the correct period and liabilities are not understated. A strong month-end close process should ask procurement, program, and operations teams whether any services were received late in the period and whether finance has not yet seen the invoice. If the answer is yes, finance should estimate the amount using available support and then reverse or clear it when the actual invoice arrives. This ensures the books reflect reality instead of waiting for paperwork to catch up. It is one of the most important cut-off controls in transaction processing.
Send to the team:
- “Please accrue received but unbilled costs at month-end using the best available evidence.”
Key point:
- Received but unbilled items should not be ignored; they belong in the period when incurred.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
36. Q: What is the role of a receiving report?
A: A receiving report is the evidence that goods or services were actually received. It usually shows the date, quantity, condition, recipient, and, where relevant, any discrepancies or shortages. This document is a key control because it confirms that the NGO is not relying only on the supplier’s invoice or on someone’s memory. In an NGO, receiving reports are especially useful for inventory, medical supplies, field equipment, and any item where physical receipt matters. They also support three-way matching and make audit testing far easier. Without receiving evidence, the organization may pay for goods it never got or may not be able to prove that value entered the project.
Send to the team:
- “Please file a receiving report for every delivery or service completion.”
Key point:
- Receiving reports prove that delivery happened and support payment and inventory decisions.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
37. Q: Why must receiving be documented?
A: Receiving must be documented so the NGO can prove what was delivered, when it arrived, and whether it matched the order. That evidence is the bridge between procurement and accounting because it tells finance when a liability or expense is justified. It also protects the organization when goods arrive damaged, incomplete, or late because the report can show the problem immediately. For inventory and asset-related transactions, documented receiving is essential to maintain custody and count records. In donor-funded work, it supports accountability by showing that purchased items really entered the program. A receiving process that is consistently filed and reviewed prevents many downstream errors from ever entering the ledger.
Send to the team:
- “Please document every receipt, even for small items, and attach the report to the payment file.”
Key point:
- Documented receiving protects completeness, accuracy, and accountability.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
38. Q: What is the receivables process in an NGO?
A: The receivables process usually starts when the NGO bills a donor, partner, customer, or other party for an amount owed. Finance then records the receivable, monitors the aging, follows up on overdue balances, matches incoming cash to the invoice or claim, and clears the open item once collection is confirmed. If the amount remains unpaid for too long, the item may need escalation, dispute review, or management action. The process matters because receivables can be a major source of cash flow strain if they are not actively managed. An NGO should not assume that because income was recognized or billed, the money is already usable. Receivables control is therefore both a recording process and a collection process.
Send to the team:
- “Please track receivables from invoice through collection and escalation.”
Key point:
- Receivables processing is billing plus collection control.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
39. Q: What causes accounts receivable in a nonprofit?
A: Accounts receivable can arise from several common nonprofit activities. These include grants or reimbursements due from donors, amounts billed to partner organizations, service income from trainings or consulting work, membership dues not yet collected, and refunds or recoveries owed to the organization. The accounting issue is not only that money is due, but also that the balance must be classified correctly so the report tells the truth about the source of the expected cash. If the NGO treats all receivables the same, it may miss important distinctions between donor-related claims and trading claims. Those differences matter for follow-up, reporting, and risk assessment. A clear receivable origin also helps management decide who should chase the balance and what documentation should be used.
Send to the team:
- “Please identify the source of each receivable clearly and keep the supporting document with the balance.”
Key point:
- Receivables arise whenever money is owed to the NGO, but the source of the claim matters.
Official links:
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
40. Q: What is the difference between a donor receivable and a customer receivable?
A: A donor receivable is an amount expected from a donor, grantor, or funding partner under the terms of a grant, contribution, or reimbursement arrangement. A customer receivable is an amount owed because the NGO sold goods or services, such as training, consulting, rent, or other chargeable activity. The difference matters because the underlying relationship affects how the amount is billed, followed up, and reported. Donor receivables often require tracking by grant agreement and restriction, while customer receivables may follow more commercial-style collection logic. They may also have different implications for revenue recognition, restricted funding schedules, and board reporting. Keeping them separate avoids confusion and supports cleaner financial analysis.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate donor-related balances from trading receivables in the schedules and reports.”
Key point:
- Donor receivables come from funding arrangements; customer receivables come from sales or services.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
41. Q: How should grant receivables be tracked?
A: Grant receivables should be tracked by donor, grant name, installment or tranche, due date, invoice or claim reference, condition status, and collection status. That level of detail helps finance understand not just how much is outstanding, but why it is outstanding and what action is needed next. In many NGOs, a grant receivable schedule is one of the most important control tools because it connects donor agreements to cash flow and reporting. If a balance is conditional, the conditions should be visible; if it is overdue, that should also be visible. The schedule should be reviewed with program staff so that finance is not managing the balance in isolation. Good grant receivable tracking supports both accountability and forecasting.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a grant receivable schedule with due dates, conditions, and collection status.”
Key point:
- Grant receivables need donor-level tracking and condition visibility.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
42. Q: What is an aging schedule for receivables?
A: An aging schedule groups receivables by the length of time they have been outstanding, usually into current and older time buckets. This lets management see which balances are fresh and which are becoming slow to collect. For an NGO, the aging schedule is not just a report for finance; it helps leadership evaluate donor delays, partner performance, and cash pressure. The report can also be used to decide which accounts need reminder letters, calls, escalation, or special agreements. A clean aging schedule should tie back to the detailed receivable records so the total can be trusted. When used well, it turns a list of open balances into a management tool.
Send to the team:
- “Please prepare the receivables aging before month-end review and use it to plan follow-up.”
Key point:
- Aging shows how old each amount is due and where collection effort should focus.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
43. Q: Why should receivables aging be reviewed?
A: Receivables aging should be reviewed because overdue balances often reveal cash risk, disputes, stalled billing, or weak collection discipline. The review helps management decide whether a receivable is still collectible, whether the cause is documentation or negotiation, and whether additional follow-up is needed. It also helps the NGO avoid carrying old balances without explanation, which can make the balance sheet less credible. Regular review improves cash forecasting because finance can separate balances likely to arrive soon from those that may not. In grant-funded environments, it also gives program and finance teams a chance to work together on unresolved claims before reporting deadlines pass. The value is not just in the numbers, but in the action the numbers trigger.
Send to the team:
- “Please review overdue receivables, update the follow-up status, and escalate stale items.”
Key point:
- Aging review supports collection, risk control, and forecasting.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
44. Q: How should overdue receivables be followed up?
A: Overdue receivables should be followed up through a structured sequence rather than through random reminders. A practical approach is to send a reminder, make a phone call or meeting request, log the response, escalate to management if needed, and agree a revised settlement plan if there is a genuine delay. Every step should be documented so there is evidence of the organization’s effort to collect. This matters because receivables that are not chased can quietly become bad debts or long-term disputes. A well-documented follow-up file also helps the NGO show auditors and donors that it is actively managing its resources. Follow-up is therefore part of internal control, not just customer service.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep a contact log for every overdue receivable and update it after each follow-up action.”
Key point:
- Overdue balances need structured follow-up and a visible audit trail.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
45. Q: What is the role of reminder letters in receivable collection?
A: Reminder letters create a formal written record that the payer has been told an amount is due or overdue. They help move collection from informal memory to documented process, which is important when multiple people are involved or when the balance has been outstanding for a long time. A good reminder letter usually identifies the invoice or claim, the amount due, the due date, and the next step expected from the payer. In an NGO, this kind of communication supports professionalism and consistency while protecting the organization’s cash position. It also reduces misunderstandings because the message is in writing rather than left to verbal memory. The letter trail becomes useful evidence if the matter later requires escalation or dispute review.
Send to the team:
- “Please send reminder letters for overdue balances and keep copies in the receivable file.”
Key point:
- Reminder letters support collection discipline and documentation.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
46. Q: What is the difference between a receivable and deferred income?
A: A receivable is an amount owed to the NGO that is expected to be collected. Deferred income is money received in advance, or sometimes billed in advance depending on the arrangement, before the related service is earned or before conditions are satisfied. The distinction matters because the organization should not record income too early just because cash has arrived. In many nonprofit settings, donor restrictions, service performance obligations, or grant conditions determine whether the amount should sit as deferred revenue rather than earned revenue. Mixing the two can distort both period results and donor reporting. Good classification ensures the balance sheet and income statement tell the same story about timing and obligation.
Send to the team:
- “Please do not treat advance income as earned revenue until the related performance or condition is satisfied.”
Key point:
- Receivable means due; deferred income means not yet earned.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
47. Q: How should an NGO record an invoice sent to a partner or buyer?
A: When the NGO sends an invoice, it should generally record a receivable and then decide what the offset should be based on the underlying arrangement. If the invoice relates to services delivered, the offset may be revenue. If it relates to reimbursement or a cost-sharing arrangement, the offset may be a recoverable amount or a due-from balance. If it is part of an inter-entity relationship, it may require separate tracking so the balance can later be reconciled. The key is not to assume every invoice is the same. The substance of the arrangement determines the accounting treatment, not the fact that a document was sent. Clear classification also makes collection and reporting more reliable.
Send to the team:
- “Please classify the invoice by transaction type before posting and make the supporting agreement visible.”
Key point:
- Invoice posting depends on the underlying arrangement, not just the document itself.
Official links:
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
48. Q: What is a receipt voucher?
A: A receipt voucher is an internal record showing that cash or another form of receipt has been received by the NGO. It usually identifies who paid, how much was received, when it was received, what it was for, and which receipt or bank reference proves the event. In control terms, it is the evidence that connects money received to the accounting records and to the correct source transaction. This is useful when receipts are collected at the front desk, in the field, or through multiple channels such as cash, bank transfer, or mobile money. A receipt voucher helps prevent cash from being received but not recorded. It also supports reconciliation and donor or customer follow-up where needed.
Send to the team:
- “Please issue a receipt voucher for each cash collection and file it with the related support.”
Key point:
- Receipt vouchers document money received and link it to the ledger.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
49. Q: How should cash receipts be matched to invoices?
A: Cash receipts should be matched to invoices using identifying details such as payer name, invoice number, amount, date, remittance note, reference code, or other supporting evidence. The matching should happen before the receipt is cleared from suspense or unapplied cash so the organization does not misstate what remains outstanding. If the amount covers only part of the invoice, the open balance should remain visible. If one receipt covers several invoices, the allocation should be documented carefully so the collection record remains traceable. This is important in nonprofits because cash may arrive from donors, partners, customers, or grantors, each with different reporting implications. Proper matching keeps the ledger clean and helps avoid manual confusion later.
Send to the team:
- “Please match every receipt to the related invoice or balance due before clearing it.”
Key point:
- Receipt matching prevents misposting and unmanaged suspense items.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://www.irs.gov/publications/p557
50. Q: What should happen when a receipt cannot be matched immediately?
A: An unmatched receipt should be parked in suspense or unapplied cash, investigated promptly, and matched only when the correct source is confirmed. The organization should resist the temptation to force the amount into the wrong account just to make the balance disappear. Instead, finance should use the bank reference, payer details, communication logs, and any remittance information to identify the source. If necessary, program staff, donor relations, or the receiving unit should be asked whether they recognize the payment. This is a control issue because unresolved receipts can mask errors, create false income, or hide collection problems. A disciplined suspense process allows the NGO to be accurate now and tidy later without sacrificing traceability.
Send to the team:
- “Please hold unmatched receipts in suspense until the source is confirmed and document every follow-up step.”
Key point:
- Unmatched receipts need investigation, not guesswork.
Official links:
- https://www.gao.gov/greenbook
- https://storage.fasb.org/ASU_2016-14.pdf
51. Q: What is a receipt allocation process?
A: A receipt allocation process is the controlled method used to assign an incoming cash receipt, transfer, or other inflow to the correct customer, donor, grant, invoice, deposit, or ledger account. It is the point where money received becomes a properly matched accounting item rather than an unidentified inflow. In practice, the process should begin with the source reference, compare the receipt to outstanding balances, identify the correct open item, and then post the amount in a way that preserves the audit trail. This matters because one receipt may settle part of an invoice, clear a donor receivable, offset an advance, or create a credit balance if it is not applied correctly. When the allocation is weak, the organization can end up with false overdue balances, unclear donor schedules, and misleading cash reporting. A good process makes the receipt traceable, explainable, and reviewable from source to ledger to reconciliation.
Send to the team:
- “Please allocate every receipt to the correct account, project, or outstanding balance before posting.”
Key point:
- Receipt allocation makes incoming money traceable and correctly matched.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
52. Q: Why do NGOs need to allocate receipts correctly?
A: NGOs need correct receipt allocation because incoming funds often have a specific purpose, donor link, invoice reference, or settlement relationship that must be preserved in the books. If the receipt is not allocated to the right item, one balance may be cleared while another remains incorrectly open, which creates reporting errors and follow-up problems. This is particularly important in nonprofit environments where donor reporting, project tracking, and cash monitoring are all tied to transaction-level accuracy. Correct allocation also helps the finance team understand whether the amount received was unrestricted cash, a restricted grant receipt, a customer settlement, or an advance that still needs recognition later. Without this discipline, the ledger can appear to balance while the underlying detail is wrong. Allocation accuracy therefore protects both the financial statements and operational follow-up.
Send to the team:
- “Please match every receipt to the correct source and outstanding item.”
Key point:
- Correct allocation protects reporting accuracy.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
53. Q: How should partial payments be recorded?
A: Partial payments should be posted against the specific invoice, advance, or receivable they relate to, while leaving the remaining balance open and visible. The accounting record should show the original amount, the amount received, the balance still due, and any note explaining why only part of the amount was settled. This helps the organization avoid falsely closing the item and gives staff a clear basis for follow-up. Partial payments are common in grant reimbursements, installment arrangements, disputed bills, and customer settlements, so the ledger must show the truth of what has and has not been paid. A clean process also prevents confusion when aging reports are reviewed or when later receipts arrive against the same item. Partial settlement should reduce the balance, not hide it.
Send to the team:
- “Please post partial payments against the correct open item and leave the balance visible.”
Key point:
- Partial payments should reduce the open balance, not close it incorrectly.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
54. Q: How should underpayments be handled?
A: Underpayments should be treated as exceptions until the reason is understood. The finance team should determine whether the short amount came from a deduction, a bank charge, a pricing disagreement, a posting mistake, a grant withholding, or a simple processing error. The unpaid portion should remain open until the issue is resolved and documented. This is important because an underpayment can signal a recoverable balance, a dispute that needs escalation, or an accounting adjustment that has not yet been recorded. If underpayments are quietly accepted, the receivable or payable balance may be wrong, and the organization can lose money without realizing it. Good practice is to investigate first, then settle or adjust with clear evidence.
Send to the team:
- “Please investigate every underpayment before closing the receivable or payable.”
Key point:
- Underpayments need explanation and follow-up.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
55. Q: How should overpayments be handled?
A: Overpayments should be identified quickly and resolved according to policy and the underlying agreement. Depending on the circumstances, the amount may be refunded, reallocated, credited to a future invoice, or held as an advance until it can be applied properly. The important control point is that the overpayment should not remain uncleared in the ledger without explanation. That could hide a duplicate payment, an incorrect receipt allocation, or a vendor or customer account problem. A strong process reviews the overpayment against supporting documents, documents the chosen resolution, and updates the ledger so the balance no longer sits in suspense. In short, overpayments must be closed deliberately, not ignored.
Send to the team:
- “Please review every overpayment and decide whether to refund, reclassify, or carry it as credit.”
Key point:
- Overpayments must be cleared properly.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
56. Q: What is a credit balance in receivables?
A: A credit balance in receivables means the ledger shows more has been credited or received than is currently due from the customer, donor, or payer. In practical terms, it may represent an advance payment, an overpayment, a deposit, or an allocation error that has not yet been corrected. Credit balances matter because they change the meaning of the account: instead of showing money owed to the organization, they may show money the organization owes back or still needs to apply. The finance team should therefore review them carefully rather than treating them as harmless leftovers. A credit balance should be explained, supported, and classified correctly so that the account is not misread during reporting or reconciliation.
Send to the team:
- “Please review all credit balances in receivables and classify them correctly.”
Key point:
- A credit balance usually means money is owed back or held in advance.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
57. Q: Why do credit balances appear in receivables?
A: Credit balances appear when receipts are posted before invoices, when payments exceed billed amounts, when adjustments are recorded, or when the same transaction is entered more than once and later partially reversed. They may also arise when donor or customer funds are received in advance of service delivery or grant expenditure. Each cause has a different accounting meaning, so the team should not assume every credit balance is the same. Instead, the reason should be identified and documented: advance, overpayment, correction, or misposting. Once the reason is known, the finance team can decide whether the amount needs refunding, reclassification, carry-forward, or reversal. Unexplained credits are a sign that the transaction process needs review.
Send to the team:
- “Please identify why each receivable credit balance exists and clear it properly.”
Key point:
- Credit balances usually come from advance, overpayment, or posting errors.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
58. Q: What is transaction coding?
A: Transaction coding is the assignment of a financial entry to the correct account code, cost center, project, donor, fund, department, or other classification field in the accounting system. It is the process that tells the ledger what the transaction represents and how it should appear in reports. Good coding ensures that the same item can be analyzed by account type, budget line, program, and funding source without rework later. In NGO work, this is especially important because many transactions affect donor reporting, project budgets, and management dashboards at the same time. A well-coded transaction supports traceability, comparison, and control. Poor coding may still post the transaction, but it places it in the wrong place and creates reporting noise.
Send to the team:
- “Please code each transaction completely and consistently before posting.”
Key point:
- Coding determines how the transaction appears in reports.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
59. Q: Why is correct coding important in transaction processing?
A: Correct coding is important because nearly every downstream report depends on it. If a transaction is coded to the wrong project, account, donor, or cost center, the budget comparison will be distorted, the donor report may be inaccurate, and the close process may require manual correction later. One coding error can also affect multiple views of the same transaction, including program cost analysis, support cost allocation, and grant tracking. Correct coding gives management confidence that the entry was deliberately placed in the right reporting bucket. It also reduces the chance that month-end close becomes a cleanup exercise for preventable errors. In short, coding quality is report quality.
Send to the team:
- “Please verify the coding before final approval and posting.”
Key point:
- Wrong coding creates wrong reports.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
60. Q: What is the role of the chart of accounts in processing transactions?
A: The chart of accounts is the official structure used to classify transactions into assets, liabilities, net assets, income, expenses, and other reporting categories. In an NGO, it often also needs to support fund tracking, project tracking, donor tracking, and location or activity tracking. A good chart of accounts does more than label entries; it creates a common language for the finance team so that every transaction is coded the same way every time. This makes reporting easier, closing faster, and review more reliable. When the chart of accounts is too weak or too generic, staff begin inventing workarounds, and the same transaction can be classified differently by different people.
Send to the team:
- “Please use the chart of accounts exactly as approved for every transaction.”
Key point:
- The chart of accounts is the base coding structure.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
61. Q: What is the role of cost centers in transaction processing?
A: Cost centers identify where the cost was incurred and which unit, department, office, or function is responsible for it. They help management see how resources are being used across the organization rather than only in total. For example, the same expense type may behave differently in headquarters, a field office, or a project site, so the cost center gives context to the amount. This makes cost center coding important for internal review, budget monitoring, and accountability. It also supports better allocation where a shared service cost needs to be distributed later. Without the cost center, the ledger loses useful operational detail.
Send to the team:
- “Please code each expense to the correct cost center.”
Key point:
- Cost centers show responsibility and location of spend.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
62. Q: What is the role of project codes in transaction processing?
A: Project codes identify the specific program, grant, activity, or intervention that a transaction belongs to. They are essential in NGO finance because a single organization may run many projects under different donors, budgets, and reporting deadlines. Without project codes, it becomes difficult to assign spending to the right grant report, budget, or output summary. Project codes also help the organization monitor spending against approved activities and keep program and finance records aligned. A well-managed project code structure gives the team a clean way to see what was spent where and why. It is one of the main controls linking the books to the mission work.
Send to the team:
- “Please assign the correct project code before posting any project-related cost or receipt.”
Key point:
- Project codes connect transactions to the right activity.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
63. Q: What is a source document?
A: A source document is the original evidence that supports a financial transaction. It may be an invoice, receipt, contract, bank slip, delivery note, approved form, staff claim, donor letter, or other record that explains why the entry exists. The source document is important because it ties the accounting entry to a real event and gives the transaction meaning beyond the numbers alone. In a good system, the document is attached, indexed, or otherwise linked to the ledger entry so that a reviewer can understand the transaction without guessing. If the document is missing or unclear, the entry becomes harder to defend and review.
Send to the team:
- “Please attach the source document to every transaction record.”
Key point:
- A source document is the proof behind the entry.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
64. Q: Why must source documents be retained?
A: Source documents must be retained so the organization can later verify what happened, who approved it, when it happened, and why it was recorded. Retention supports audit work, management review, donor accountability, dispute resolution, and correction of errors that are discovered after posting. If a transaction needs to be checked months later, the supporting evidence must still be available and readable. A finance system without retention discipline may still post entries, but it cannot reliably defend them later. Good retention means the documents are filed securely, can be retrieved quickly, and are kept for the full retention period under policy.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep supporting documents available for the full retention period.”
Key point:
- Retained evidence makes transactions auditable.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
65. Q: What is a filing system for AP and AR?
A: A filing system for accounts payable and accounts receivable is the organized method used to store invoices, remittances, approvals, statements, receipts, correspondence, and follow-up evidence. The purpose is to make each transaction easy to find by reference number, date, vendor, customer, project, or period. Good filing supports the AP and AR workflow by reducing search time, avoiding duplicate handling, and keeping the documentary trail complete. It also helps with close, audit, and dispute handling because the team can produce the required evidence without rebuilding the history of the item. A filing system is not just storage; it is a control tool that supports traceability and accountability.
Send to the team:
- “Please file AP and AR records in a way that matches the transaction reference and period.”
Key point:
- Good filing makes AP and AR traceable.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
66. Q: How should supporting documents be archived?
A: Supporting documents should be archived in a secure, organized, and retrievable system with clear naming, indexing, and retention rules. The archive should protect records from loss, tampering, accidental deletion, and staff turnover. A good archive uses a structure that reflects the reporting period, transaction type, and owner so users can find what they need quickly. Whether the archive is physical or digital, the control objective is the same: the evidence must still be usable when the transaction is reviewed later. A document that cannot be found is not effectively archived, even if it was saved somewhere in theory.
Send to the team:
- “Please archive documents using a standard folder and naming structure.”
Key point:
- Archiving should preserve both security and retrieval speed.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
67. Q: What is a transaction log?
A: A transaction log is a running record of financial items received, reviewed, posted, corrected, followed up, or closed. It shows the status of each item as it moves through the processing cycle and helps the team see what is open, what is resolved, and what still needs action. A good transaction log turns work that might otherwise sit in inboxes or memory into a visible process. It is especially useful for AP and AR because it highlights outstanding items, missing documents, and pending approvals. When kept current, the log becomes a practical management tool for monitoring workload and exceptions.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep the transaction log current for all open and completed items.”
Key point:
- A transaction log tracks the life of each item.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
68. Q: Why should transaction logs be kept current?
A: Transaction logs should be kept current because their value depends on showing the real status of the work right now, not last week. If the log is updated late, unresolved items may be missed, follow-up can be delayed, and open balances may age without anyone noticing. A current log helps staff identify bottlenecks, track ownership, and confirm whether the issue is still pending or already resolved. It also supports close and audit readiness by showing that exceptions are being monitored systematically. In effect, the log should behave like a live control, not a historical file. The more current it is, the more useful it becomes.
Send to the team:
- “Please update the log daily or as soon as a transaction changes status.”
Key point:
- Current logs support timely follow-up.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
69. Q: What is the difference between manual and system-generated entries?
A: Manual entries are made directly by a person, while system-generated entries are created automatically by the accounting system based on configured rules or linked processes. Manual entries generally require more scrutiny because they depend more heavily on human judgment and are more exposed to error, override, or inconsistent treatment. System-generated entries may be more repeatable, but they still need review to confirm the logic, source data, and mapping are correct. The key issue is not that one type is always better, but that each carries different risks. Manual entries usually need extra review because they can be adjusted without the protections built into automated workflows.
Send to the team:
- “Please mark manual entries clearly and review them before posting.”
Key point:
- Manual entries need more scrutiny than system-generated ones.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
70. Q: Why should manual entries be reviewed more carefully?
A: Manual entries should be reviewed more carefully because they bypass some of the controls that normally exist in structured transaction workflows. They can affect multiple accounts, reverse prior postings, or hide timing issues if they are not independently checked. This makes them higher risk than routine system postings, especially at month end when staff are rushing to close. Review should confirm the accounting logic, the mathematical accuracy, the support, and the approval. Manual entries are not inherently bad; they are simply the kind of item that needs stronger evidence and independent checking before posting.
Send to the team:
- “Please require independent review for all manual journal entries.”
Key point:
- Manual entries are higher-risk and need independent checking.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
71. Q: What is the role of journal vouchers in transaction processing?
A: Journal vouchers document non-routine entries such as corrections, reallocations, accruals, reversals, and period-end adjustments. Their purpose is to explain why the entry is needed, what accounts are affected, who prepared it, who approved it, and what evidence supports it. This is important because a manual adjustment may be technically correct but still unclear if the explanation is weak. In NGO finance, journal vouchers are especially useful where donor reporting, grant reconciliation, or project cost allocation depends on a clear audit trail. A strong journal voucher process turns judgment-based postings into controlled records.
Send to the team:
- “Please use a journal voucher form for every manual adjustment.”
Key point:
- Journal vouchers explain manual ledger changes.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
72. Q: What is the difference between a journal entry and a cash payment?
A: A journal entry is an accounting record, while a cash payment is the actual movement of money through the bank or cash account. A journal entry may record an accrual, reclassification, correction, or revenue adjustment without any cash changing hands. A cash payment, by contrast, settles an obligation and affects liquidity immediately. Confusing the two leads to bad reporting because the books may show a transaction before or after cash moves, depending on the accounting basis and the nature of the item. Good processing keeps the accounting logic and the cash movement separate so both the ledger and the bank can be understood clearly.
Send to the team:
- “Please distinguish accounting adjustments from actual cash payments.”
Key point:
- Not every journal entry is a cash movement.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
73. Q: How should transaction cut-off be handled at month end?
A: Month-end cut-off should ensure transactions are recorded in the correct period based on when the service was received, the obligation was incurred, the cash was received, or the goods were delivered, depending on the accounting basis and policy. Near close, the team should review late invoices, unposted receipts, delivery notes, accrual items, and any other transaction that may belong to the current month rather than the next. The purpose is to prevent period drift, where activity is pushed into the wrong month simply because paperwork arrived late. Good cut-off work requires communication with program staff, procurement, and cash handlers because finance cannot judge timing correctly without timely source evidence. Close procedures should always include cut-off review as a specific step.
Send to the team:
- “Please review all late AP, AR, and adjustment items before closing the month.”
Key point:
- Cut-off ensures the period reflects the right activity.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
74. Q: What is cut-off risk in AP and AR?
A: Cut-off risk is the risk that invoices, receipts, goods received, or services delivered are recorded in the wrong accounting period. In AP, that can mean expenses and liabilities are understated because invoices arrived late and were not accrued. In AR, it can mean receipts or billings are recorded too early or too late, which distorts balances and revenue recognition. Cut-off problems often appear at busy month-end closes when staff are trying to process many items quickly and may rely on incomplete documents or assumptions. The impact is not only timing error; it can also distort donor reports, management commentary, and the statement of financial position. A good process checks timing before it checks final totals.
Send to the team:
- “Please check cut-off carefully for all AP and AR close items.”
Key point:
- Cut-off errors shift activity into the wrong period.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
75. Q: What is an accrual in transaction processing?
A: An accrual is an entry that recognizes income or expense in the period to which it belongs, even when cash has not yet been received or paid. It exists to align the books with economic reality rather than only with the bank statement. In practice, accruals are used for services received but not yet invoiced, expenses incurred but not yet settled, and other timing differences that would otherwise leave the month incomplete. Without accruals, the ledger can look cleaner than it really is because obligations and earned amounts were delayed rather than recognized. Proper accrual processing is therefore a core part of accurate month-end reporting.
Send to the team:
- “Please accrue items that belong to the month even if cash is not yet settled.”
Key point:
- Accruals capture period-end timing differences.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
76. Q: What is a prepayment in transaction processing?
A: A prepayment is cash paid in advance for a future good, service, or obligation that has not yet been fully received or consumed. At the time of payment, it is usually not yet an expense because the organization still expects future benefit from it. The accounting treatment should therefore keep the amount visible as an asset or advance until the benefit is used, delivered, or expired. Prepayments matter because organizations often pay rent, subscriptions, services, insurance, or vendor deposits in advance, and these amounts should not disappear into current expense too early. A good process tracks the benefit period rather than only the payment date.
Send to the team:
- “Please record advance payments as prepayments until the benefit is received.”
Key point:
- A prepayment is an asset until used or consumed.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
77. Q: How should advance payments to vendors be handled at close?
A: Advance payments to vendors should be reviewed at close to confirm whether the goods or services have actually been received. If they have not, the amount should remain as a prepayment or vendor advance rather than being charged fully to expense. This protects the period’s results from being overstated by costs that do not yet belong there. The finance team should also check the underlying contract or purchase order to confirm the expected timing of delivery and any conditions attached to the advance. Older vendor advances should be monitored in a schedule so they do not remain open without explanation. Good close procedures therefore distinguish paid from consumed.
Send to the team:
- “Please review all vendor advances and classify them correctly at month end.”
Key point:
- Vendor advances usually remain on the balance sheet until used.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
78. Q: How should advance receipts from customers or donors be handled at close?
A: Advance receipts should be reviewed to determine whether they are deferred revenue, a liability, a restricted receipt, or another balance that should not yet be recognized as income. The key question is whether the organization has already earned the amount or still owes a service, program obligation, or release condition. If the delivery requirement or condition has not occurred, the receipt should remain unearned or deferred rather than being treated as current revenue. This is especially important in NGO settings where funds may arrive before implementation begins or before grant conditions are satisfied. Proper treatment avoids overstating income and helps the balance sheet reflect what the organization still owes or must deliver.
Send to the team:
- “Please assess all advance receipts before recognizing revenue.”
Key point:
- Advance receipts are often liabilities until earned or released.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
79. Q: What is the role of aging reports in working capital control?
A: Aging reports show how long payables and receivables have been outstanding, which makes them one of the most practical working capital tools in finance. They highlight overdue supplier balances, delayed customer payments, old advances, and accounts that need escalation or correction. By separating current items from older ones, the report shows where cash pressure may be building and where collection or settlement efforts need attention. Aging also helps managers see whether credit policies, payment terms, or follow-up routines are working. In a disciplined process, aging reports are reviewed regularly and used to drive action rather than simply filed after close.
Send to the team:
- “Please review aging reports regularly and follow up on overdue balances.”
Key point:
- Aging reports help manage timing and liquidity.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
80. Q: How do payables affect cash planning?
A: Payables affect cash planning because they show the obligations that will likely need to be paid in the near future. A finance team that only looks at the current bank balance may miss the fact that large invoices, payroll, tax items, or supplier payments are due soon. The result can be a liquidity surprise even when the books appear stable. Tracking payables in the cash forecast helps management decide whether to delay nonessential spending, accelerate receipts, or arrange temporary funding. In an NGO, this is especially important because program commitments and donor restrictions may limit how cash can be used.
Send to the team:
- “Please include open payables in the weekly cash forecast.”
Key point:
- Payables are future cash outflows.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
81. Q: How do receivables affect cash planning?
A: Receivables affect cash planning because they represent money expected in the future, not money already in hand. If receivables are old, disputed, or weakly followed up, the forecast can become too optimistic and the organization may plan spending against cash that will not arrive on time. Good cash planning therefore distinguishes between amounts expected, amounts likely to be collected, and amounts that are already overdue or doubtful. This distinction is especially important for NGOs that depend on donor reimbursements, milestone billing, or customer settlements to fund operations. A strong forecast is built from receivable reality, not from the face value of the ledger alone.
Send to the team:
- “Please track expected collections and compare them to actual receipts.”
Key point:
- Receivables are expected cash inflows, not cash in hand.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
82. Q: What is working capital in an NGO context?
A: Working capital in an NGO context is the short-term resource cushion that allows the organization to keep operating while waiting for receipts and while settling current obligations. It is commonly understood as the difference between current assets and current liabilities, but in practice it is also a signal about timing pressure and operational flexibility. If current liabilities are growing faster than current assets, the organization may face stress in paying vendors, meeting payroll, or starting the next project phase. If receivables are slow or advances are locked, working capital may be tighter than the profit-and-loss statement suggests. For NGOs, working capital is therefore a practical measure of resilience, not just a textbook ratio.
Send to the team:
- “Please monitor current assets and current liabilities together when reviewing liquidity.”
Key point:
- Working capital supports short-term operating health.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO internal control standards
83. Q: How should an NGO monitor payment terms with suppliers?
A: An NGO should monitor supplier payment terms by tracking due dates, agreed credit periods, discount windows, and any conditions attached to the invoice or contract. This helps the organization avoid late fees, maintain supplier trust, and manage cash intentionally rather than reactively.
