- FX differences can change reported performance.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
13. Q: What is a foreign currency bank account?
A: It is a bank account maintained in a currency different from the NGO’s functional currency. The amount in the bank statement is therefore not yet the amount that will appear in the ledger or reporting pack in functional currency terms. Because the account is exposed to exchange movement, its value in the books can change even if no cash is deposited or withdrawn.

That is why foreign currency bank accounts need both bank reconciliation and FX retranslation at period end. A clean close process should identify the balance in account currency, the rate used for translation, and the resulting functional-currency balance. This is especially important where grants are held in a donor currency while local spending is done in another currency. The bank account is not just a place to keep cash; it is also a live FX exposure that needs routine review.
Send to the team:
- “Please track the bank balance in both account currency and functional currency.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency bank accounts still need FX treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
14. Q: How should a foreign currency bank account be reconciled?
A: First reconcile the bank statement to the ledger in the account currency so all receipts, payments, charges, and transfers are matched. Then translate the reconciled ending balance into the functional currency using the closing rate and compare that value to the ledger carrying amount. Any difference created by exchange movement should be recorded clearly as an FX difference, not hidden inside unexplained reconciling items.

This gives the organization one clean reconciliation that covers both cash movement and currency movement. It also makes it easier to see whether the difference arose from timing, bank charges, transfer lag, or genuine FX change. A solid monthly close will also check that foreign bank balances agree across the cashbook, bank reconciliation, treasury schedule, and trial balance. That is the point where cash control, FX control, and reporting control meet in one document.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile the foreign currency bank account in both currencies and attach the rate used at month end.”
Key point:
- Reconciliation must cover both the bank and the FX translation.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
15. Q: What is the risk of holding cash in multiple currencies?
A: The risk is that the functional value of cash can change before the organization uses it, so the purchasing power of the balance may fall or rise without any movement in the bank. This can create liquidity pressure even when the bank account still looks healthy in nominal terms. A team may believe it has enough funds to complete a program, only to find that exchange movement has reduced the local value available for spending.

Multi-currency cash therefore needs tighter planning, not just passive reporting. The liquidity dashboard should show balances in both foreign currency and functional currency so the finance team can see real spend capacity. Where the NGO has several operating currencies, management should also know which balances are earmarked for near-term commitments and which are still exposed to rate movement. That makes treasury a planning function, not only an administrative one.
Send to the team:
- “Please monitor multi-currency cash positions daily or weekly and show the functional-currency equivalent.”
Key point:
- Multi-currency cash creates valuation risk.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
16. Q: How should an NGO monitor currency risk?
A: It should track all foreign currency cash, receivables, payables, advances, loans, and grant balances, then compare those exposures against expected inflows and outflows by currency. The purpose is not merely to list balances, but to understand the direction and timing of the risk. If the NGO expects to pay a supplier in three months but holds the money in a different currency today, the exposure needs to be visible well before settlement.

Regular monitoring also gives management time to decide whether to accelerate spending, convert cash, renegotiate payment timing, or update forecasts. A practical FX exposure report should show currency, balance type, due date, and whether the item is an asset or liability. That report should sit alongside the cash forecast and grant utilization tracker so the organization can see both accounting exposure and operational impact. The goal is not to eliminate all FX risk, but to understand it early enough to act.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a monthly FX exposure report by currency, due date, and balance type.”
Key point:
- Currency risk should be measured by exposure.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
17. Q: What is currency exposure?
A: Currency exposure is the amount of value that could change because the organization must receive or pay money in a foreign currency. It exists whenever the NGO has an open foreign currency asset or liability and is not yet fully settled. Exposure is what turns ordinary transactions into financial risk, because the final local-currency value is still uncertain until the item is closed.

The larger the exposure and the longer it remains open, the more likely the FX effect will matter to the budget and reported results. Exposure is therefore a management concept as much as an accounting one. It tells leadership where value may move before settlement and where control attention should be concentrated. A clean exposure schedule can also help treasury prioritize which balances should be paid, converted, or monitored more closely.
Send to the team:
- “Please measure exposure by currency, balance type, and due date.”
Key point:
- Exposure is the amount affected by FX movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
18. Q: Why is currency exposure important for budgeting?
A: It matters because the local-currency cost of activities can change before the NGO spends the money, which can create overspending or under-spending even when the original donor budget looked sufficient. This is especially true for imports, travel, payroll support, and cross-border procurement. A budget without FX sensitivity can give a false sense of certainty.

A better budget includes a base rate and one or more stress scenarios so managers can see what happens if the currency moves against them. For NGO planning, FX sensitivity should be treated as part of scenario design, not as an unusual exception. That makes budget review more realistic and helps leadership understand whether a shortfall is caused by implementation or by rate movement. It also improves donor communication when a reforecast is needed later in the year.
Send to the team:
- “Please budget with an FX sensitivity range, not one fixed rate only.”
Key point:
- Budgeting should allow for rate movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
19. Q: What is a foreign currency payable?
A: It is an obligation to pay a fixed or determinable amount in a foreign currency, such as an unpaid supplier invoice, a loan installment, or a service fee owed to a cross-border vendor. Because it is a monetary liability, its functional-currency value can move before payment is made. That means the balance on the books may change from one reporting date to the next even though the original invoice amount remains the same.

The finance team needs to know both the original foreign amount and the current functional amount so the obligation is not understated or overstated. Foreign currency payables should be aged and remeasured together so the team can see both settlement timing and FX movement. In an NGO environment, this is especially important for procurement and field operations, where delayed payment can change the local value of a budget line quickly. The payable schedule should therefore show amount, currency, vendor, due date, and revaluation status.
Send to the team:
- “Please list all foreign currency payables separately from local currency payables.”
Key point:
- Payables in other currencies must be remeasured.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
20. Q: What is a foreign currency receivable?
A: It is an amount the NGO expects to receive in a foreign currency, such as a donor receivable, a grant reimbursement, a customer invoice, or a refund due from a supplier. Because it is an asset rather than a liability, exchange movement can increase or decrease the amount of functional currency that will ultimately be collected. This makes the receivable more than a simple admin balance; it is also a value that can shift before settlement.

The organization should therefore monitor aging, due date, and exchange movement together instead of treating them as separate topics. A receivable that looks current in foreign currency terms may still be exposed to a meaningful FX change in functional currency terms. The practical control is to keep the open-item listing current, remeasure it at close, and compare it with collection expectations. That way the finance team can distinguish a slow-paying counterparty from a genuine exchange movement effect.
Send to the team:
- “Please track foreign currency receivables by currency, aging, and expected collection date.”
Key point:
- Receivables in other currencies also need FX treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
21. Q: How should foreign currency payables be measured?
A: They should be measured initially at the transaction-date spot rate and then remeasured at each reporting date using the closing rate until the obligation is settled. The same invoice can therefore have more than one functional-currency value during its life on the books. That is normal and expected for a monetary liability. The important control is that the remeasurement is performed consistently and the resulting exchange difference is recorded in the right period.

If the liability is long outstanding, the FX impact can become material enough to distort both the balance sheet and the operating result if it is not kept up to date. The finance team should therefore keep a monthly revaluation schedule, tie it to the subledger, and make sure every open foreign-currency liability is on the close checklist. The accounting result should reflect the most current obligation, not an outdated historical rate. This is especially important when donor grants are budgeted in one currency but vendor payments are in another.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue all open foreign currency payables at month end and retain the rate evidence.”
Key point:
- Payables follow transaction-date then closing-rate measurement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
22. Q: How should foreign currency receivables be measured?
A: They should be measured initially at the transaction-date spot rate and then remeasured at the closing rate at each reporting date until collected. This ensures the receivable reflects the amount the NGO is actually expecting to realize in functional currency terms at period end. If exchange rates change materially, the receivable value can move up or down even when the invoice amount has not changed.

That is why the receivables aging report and the FX revaluation schedule should be reviewed together during close. The control focus is on completeness, timing, and the correct closing rate for open items. Where donor reimbursements or partner settlements are delayed, the receivable should remain visible on both the open-item listing and the FX exposure schedule until the cash is actually received. That makes the balance sheet and the cash forecast more reliable.
Send to the team:
- “Please update foreign currency receivables to the closing rate at period end and review the gain or loss impact.”
Key point:
- Receivables are remeasured until cash is collected.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
23. Q: What happens when a foreign currency payable is settled?
A: When the payable is settled, the liability is closed at the amount actually paid in the foreign currency, translated into functional currency at the settlement-date rate. If that amount differs from the carrying value in the books, the difference becomes a realized exchange gain or loss. This realized difference is important because it shows the final effect of currency movement on that specific obligation.

It should be recognized separately from the vendor cost itself so the organization can understand whether the variation came from operations or from FX movement. The payable should then be removed from the open-item schedule so aging and exposure reports remain accurate. In practice, the accountant should check the original invoice amount, the latest revalued amount, the cash paid, and the closing entry all in one sequence. That makes the settlement traceable and prevents the FX result from being absorbed into the operating expense line by mistake.
Send to the team:
- “Please record the settlement difference separately from the original invoice and mark the payable as closed.”
Key point:
- Settlement creates realized FX differences.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
24. Q: What happens when a foreign currency receivable is collected?
A: When the receivable is collected, the asset is closed at the amount of cash actually received in the foreign currency, translated into functional currency at the collection-date rate. If the cash received differs from the carrying amount already recorded in the books, the difference becomes a realized exchange gain or loss. This closing step matters because it finalizes the impact of currency movement on the NGO’s expected inflow.

If the organization does not compare the cash received to the latest carrying amount, it may miss the true FX effect of the collection. Once collected, the item should be removed from the open receivables schedule and the realized FX posted to the correct account. This is especially useful when donor reimbursements arrive after month end or when customer payments come in after a long delay, because the report then shows both the collection timing and the currency effect separately. That distinction improves both cash control and donor reporting.
Send to the team:
- “Please compare cash received to the remeasured receivable balance and post any realized FX difference.”
Key point:
- Collection also creates realized FX differences.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
25. Q: What is a realized exchange gain or loss?
A: It is the exchange difference recognized when a foreign currency item is actually settled or collected. The gain or loss is realized because the item is no longer open and the final currency effect has become known. This usually happens when cash changes hands and the liability or receivable disappears from the balance sheet. In reporting terms, realized FX helps managers see which differences were tied to actual settlement rather than end-of-period valuation.

For NGOs, that separation is useful because realized FX belongs to a completed transaction, while unrealized FX belongs to an open position still exposed to market movement. The finance team should show both clearly so leadership can understand whether the period result was affected by currency movement already locked in or by exposures still outstanding at month end. A good close file will therefore distinguish revaluation entries, settlement entries, and the schedule that supports them. That keeps reporting precise and auditable.
Send to the team:
- “Please post realized FX differences to the FX gain or loss account when the item is settled.”
Key point:
- Realized FX happens at settlement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
26. Q: What is an unrealized exchange gain or loss?
A: It is the exchange difference recognized before settlement, usually at the reporting date when open monetary items are remeasured to the closing rate. It is called unrealized because no cash settlement has yet occurred, even though the carrying value has changed. This difference matters in month-end and year-end close because it affects the current financial statements before the transaction is finished. The finance team should always separate unrealized FX from ordinary operating items so the close file tells a complete and accurate story. In NGO reporting, that separation helps management see whether the apparent gain or loss came from currency movement rather than from program delivery or procurement activity. If the team skips this step, the ledger can remain outdated and the reporting pack will not reflect the real value of open exposures.
Send to the team:
- “Please record period-end FX remeasurement even if no cash has moved.”
Key point:
- Unrealized FX comes from open balances at period end.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
27. Q: Why should unrealized exchange differences be reviewed at close?
A: They should be reviewed at close because they affect the carrying amount of open monetary assets and liabilities, and they can materially change the reported result for the period. If they are not reviewed, the balance sheet may be stale and the income statement may show an incomplete picture of performance. In an NGO, where grants and budgets are often monitored closely, unreviewed FX differences can make donor reports or management packs harder to trust. A close review also reduces the risk that a later settlement will expose an earlier measurement error. It is usually easier to correct an FX issue at month end than to explain a large unexplained adjustment later in the year. The review should therefore compare the revaluation schedule, the ledger, and the open-item listing item by item.
Send to the team:
- “Please review all unrealized FX balances during month-end close and reconcile them to the revaluation schedule.”
Key point:
- Close review prevents hidden FX misstatements.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
28. Q: What is the role of exchange rates in month-end close?
A: Exchange rates determine how open foreign currency monetary items are translated into the functional currency at the reporting date. In a clean close, the finance team uses an approved rate source, applies the correct closing rate, calculates the difference, and posts the revaluation entry before finalizing the trial balance. This process ensures the financial statements reflect current values rather than stale historical amounts. It also gives management confidence that the reported foreign currency position has been updated consistently across all accounts. If the rate source is not approved, or if different offices use different rates, the close can lose consistency and become difficult to audit. The exchange rate file should therefore be attached to the month-end pack and reviewed with the balance sheet reconciliation.
Send to the team:
- “Please attach the approved closing rate source to the close file and keep the revaluation workings with the trial balance.”
Key point:
- The close depends on the correct month-end rate.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
29. Q: What should be included in a foreign currency revaluation workflow?
A: The workflow should begin by identifying all open monetary balances in foreign currency, such as cash, receivables, payables, advances, and loans. It should then use the approved closing rate, recalculate the functional-currency carrying value, compare that amount to the ledger, and post the exchange gain or loss to the correct account. After posting, the finance team should reconcile the revaluation schedule to the general ledger and retain clear evidence of the rate source and calculation. This makes the process repeatable, reviewable, and defensible during audit or management review. A strong workflow also assigns ownership for preparation, review, approval, posting, and final filing so the process does not depend on one person’s memory. Where multiple entities are involved, the workflow should show which balances belong to which office or legal entity before any revaluation is done.
Send to the team:
- “Please follow one standard FX revaluation process every month and file the supporting schedule with the close pack.”
Key point:
- Revaluation needs source, calculation, posting, and reconciliation.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
30. Q: Why should foreign currency balances be revalued regularly?
A: They should be revalued regularly because the amount that matters for reporting is the current functional-currency value, not the amount recorded when the transaction first happened. If the balance stays unchanged for weeks or months, the ledger becomes increasingly disconnected from economic reality. Regular revaluation also prevents a large catch-up adjustment at year end, which can be harder to explain and review. For NGO finance, frequent revaluation helps keep budgets, donor reporting, and cash planning aligned with current rates instead of old assumptions. It also helps avoid surprises when a later settlement produces a realized gain or loss that looks unusually large simply because the open balance was not maintained during the period. Regular review is therefore both a reporting control and a forecasting control.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue all open FX balances at each reporting date, not only at year end.”
Key point:
- Regular revaluation keeps balances current.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
31. Q: What is a revaluation journal entry?
A: It is the journal entry posted to bring an open foreign currency monetary balance to the closing rate at period end and recognize the exchange difference created by that update. The entry usually adjusts either the asset or liability and posts the offset to an exchange gain or loss account. It is not a cash transaction; it is a measurement adjustment that keeps the books current. Because the entry affects both the balance sheet and the period result, it should be reviewed carefully before posting. The support should show the original foreign amount, the exchange rate used, the translated functional amount, the prior carrying amount, and the resulting difference. Without those workings, the journal entry may be correct in form but weak in evidence.
Send to the team:
- “Please post the FX revaluation entry separately from normal operating entries and keep the calculation attached.”
Key point:
- Revaluation entries capture period-end FX movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
32. Q: How does revaluation affect the financial statements?
A: Revaluation changes the carrying amount of open foreign currency monetary items on the statement of financial position and records the resulting exchange difference in the period result. That means the balance sheet becomes more current, but the profit or surplus line may also change even if no cash has moved. This is important in nonprofits because management may otherwise think the organization’s operating performance worsened when the movement was actually due to currency fluctuation. Revaluation therefore affects both the balance and the narrative that accompanies the reports. In a donor-funded environment, that narrative matters because it can influence how a variance is interpreted and whether an explanation is needed in the donor report. The finance team should therefore explain revaluation effects plainly in the management commentary.
Send to the team:
- “Please review the impact of every FX revaluation on both the balance sheet and the performance report.”
Key point:
- Revaluation affects both the balance and the result.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
33. Q: What is the accounting treatment for foreign currency cash held in bank?
A: Foreign currency cash held in bank is a monetary item and should be retranslated at the closing rate at each reporting date. Even though the cash itself does not change quantity, its value in functional currency can move because the exchange rate has changed. That movement must be recognized so the bank balance in the books matches its current translated value. If the NGO operates multiple foreign-currency accounts, each one should be reviewed separately to avoid hiding differences in a single aggregated balance. The treasury schedule should show the cash balance in account currency, the functional-currency equivalent, and the effect of retranslation at period end. This makes the cash position easier to interpret and easier to reconcile.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue foreign currency cash at month end and reconcile the translated amount to the ledger.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency cash is a monetary item.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
34. Q: What is the accounting treatment for foreign currency advances?
A: The treatment depends on whether the advance creates a monetary item or a non-monetary item. If the advance is a receivable or payable that will be settled in money, it is generally treated as a monetary balance and remeasured. If the advance relates to a prepayment for goods or services and is non-monetary in substance, the historical rate may be retained for the relevant portion. The finance team should review the underlying substance of the advance before deciding whether to revalue it, because the legal label alone is not always enough. In practice, this means the team should check the contract, the settlement terms, and the nature of what will eventually be delivered. The accounting result should follow the economic substance, not just the payment timing.
Send to the team:
- “Please determine whether each advance is monetary or non-monetary before revaluing it.”
Key point:
- The nature of the advance determines the FX treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
35. Q: What is the accounting treatment for foreign currency loans?
A: Foreign currency loans are monetary items, so they are initially recorded at the transaction-date spot rate and remeasured at the closing rate until repayment. Because loans are often large and stay open for a long period, FX movement can create meaningful differences over time. The lender still expects the same foreign amount, but the NGO’s functional-currency obligation may rise or fall. That makes foreign loans a major area for treasury, accounting, and liquidity monitoring, not just a balance sheet detail. The finance team should keep a loan-by-loan schedule that shows principal, due date, currency, rate used, and revaluation movement. If the loan is for multiple entities, the inter-entity balance and the external debt should be tracked separately.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue foreign currency loans every reporting period and explain the movement in the close file.”
Key point:
- Loans in foreign currency must be remeasured until repayment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
36. Q: What is the accounting treatment for foreign currency grants?
A: A foreign currency grant should be tracked in both the grant currency and the functional currency, with exchange differences recognized where the underlying balance is monetary. If the NGO receives funds that remain unspent, the local-currency value of those funds may change before they are used. This means grant tracking cannot stop at the donor amount alone; it must also show the translated value and any FX effect. Strong grant tracking protects both the donor restriction and the financial accuracy of the organization’s reporting. Where donor conditions are attached, the finance team should also keep the grant status, the condition status, and the exchange effect separate so the report does not blur the reason the balance remains open. That distinction is especially useful when discussing carryforward balances with a donor or board.
Send to the team:
- “Please track grant funds in both currencies and review FX effects monthly.”
Key point:
- Grant tracking must preserve both currency and purpose.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
37. Q: How should a donor-funded foreign currency grant be tracked?
A: It should be tracked by donor, purpose, budget line, original currency, exchange rate used, translated functional-currency value, and remaining balance after spending. That creates one schedule that can answer both donor questions and accounting questions without forcing the team to reconstruct the story from multiple files. It also helps the NGO explain whether any remaining amount is due to underspending, timing differences, or exchange movement. The best tracker is one that can be reconciled directly to the ledger and to the donor report. A strong tracker also flags grants that are close to expiry, underspent, or exposed to a large FX change before the next reporting deadline. When used properly, it becomes both a compliance tool and a management tool.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a grant tracker that shows both donor details and FX details in one place.”
Key point:
- Donor tracking and FX tracking must be linked.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
38. Q: How should foreign currency restrictions be monitored?
A: They should be monitored by separating donor or funding restrictions from exchange-rate effects, because those are two different kinds of limitation. A restricted balance tells the organization what the funds can be used for, while an FX difference tells it how much the local-currency value has moved since recognition. If the two are mixed together, the report stops showing which issue is caused by the donor and which is caused by the market. Clear separation makes it easier to protect compliance and to explain remaining balances accurately. This is especially important for NGOs because the same balance may be simultaneously restricted in purpose and exposed to currency movement in value. The schedule should therefore identify restriction status, open balance, and translated value separately.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate donor restriction balances from FX differences in the reports and schedules.”
Key point:
- Restriction control and FX control are different.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
39. Q: What is the difference between transaction date and reporting date exchange rates?
A: The transaction date rate is the rate used when the foreign currency item is first recorded. The reporting date rate is the closing rate used later to remeasure any open monetary balance at period end. These two dates serve different accounting purposes, so they should not be used interchangeably. Using the wrong date can distort both the initial entry and the ending balance, which in turn affects the exchange difference recognized in the period. This distinction matters most when the transaction date and the period end are far apart, because the exchange difference can become material. The accounting file should therefore show both dates clearly so anyone reviewing it can see how the balance was built.
Send to the team:
- “Please use the transaction date for initial entry and the reporting date for remeasurement.”
Key point:
- Different dates serve different accounting steps.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
40. Q: What is the average rate and when is it used?
A: An average rate is an approximation of the actual spot rate over a period such as a week or month, and it may be used when it reasonably reflects the transactions occurring in that period. It is mainly a practical tool for large volumes of similar transactions, not a shortcut for every situation. If exchange rates are very volatile, a period average may be too rough and the finance team may need more precise transaction-date rates. The key is that the approximation must be defensible and should not materially distort the ledger. Average rates are useful in NGO environments where many small field transactions occur and the administrative burden of transaction-by-transaction conversion would be excessive. Even then, the chosen method should be approved and used consistently across the organization.
Send to the team:
- “Please use average rates only when they reasonably approximate the spot rate and document the basis used.”
Key point:
- Average rates are a practical approximation, not a default.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
41. Q: What is the spot rate?
A: The spot rate is the exchange rate used for immediate conversion on a specific date, and it is the main rate used for initial recognition of foreign currency transactions. It represents the current exchange relationship at that point in time rather than an estimated or averaged value. For accounting purposes, it gives the first functional-currency amount assigned to the transaction. In NGO finance, the spot rate matters because it establishes the base from which later remeasurement gains or losses are measured. A well-run finance team should be able to show which spot rate source was used, what date it applied to, and how it was approved. That clarity avoids disputes when the ledger is later reconciled or audited.
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm the spot rate source for the transaction date before posting the entry.”
Key point:
- Spot rate is the standard starting rate.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
42. Q: What is a closing rate?
A: A closing rate is the exchange rate at the reporting date used to retranslate foreign currency monetary items at period end. It is the rate that reflects the balance’s value as of the date the financial statements are being prepared. Because the closing rate can differ from the original transaction-date rate, it may create an exchange difference that must be recognized in the current period. Using the correct closing rate is therefore essential to an accurate month-end or year-end close. The rate should be supported by an approved source and tied directly to the reporting date used in the close calendar. Without that control, the FX entries may not reconcile cleanly to the trial balance.
Send to the team:
- “Please apply the month-end closing rate to open monetary balances and keep the rate evidence in the close file.”
Key point:
- Closing rate is the period-end measurement rate.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
43. Q: What is the role of exchange rate policy in NGO finance?
A: The policy sets the approved sources of rates, the timing rules for using them, the conditions for using average rates, and the responsibilities for review and approval. Without a policy, different staff may use different rates for similar items, which creates inconsistency and weakens auditability. A good policy also explains how FX gains and losses are recorded, how often balances are revalued, and who signs off on exceptions. In that sense, the policy is not just a technical note; it is the control framework for the entire FX process. It should also explain how currency exposure is monitored, how inter-entity balances are handled, and what evidence must be retained in the close file. The policy becomes the standard reference for both headquarters and field offices.
Send to the team:
- “Please follow one approved FX policy across all teams and projects.”
Key point:
- Policy creates consistency and control.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
44. Q: What should an NGO include in an FX policy?
A: It should include functional currency rules, approved exchange rate sources, treatment of transaction-date rates, use of average rates, month-end revaluation steps, gain or loss posting rules, and review responsibilities. It should also cover how to handle foreign currency bank accounts, advances, loans, and any exceptions that may arise during period close. The most useful policy is one that removes uncertainty for staff and creates a clear audit trail for every conversion decision. That way the team can move quickly while still staying disciplined. It should state who prepares the FX schedule, who reviews the calculations, who approves the journal entry, and where the documentation is filed. Where multiple countries are involved, the policy should also define how entity-specific functional currencies are established and reviewed.
Send to the team:
- “Please document the approved FX policy, share it with finance staff, and apply it consistently.”
Key point:
- FX policy should cover measurement, posting, and review.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
45. Q: Who should approve exchange rate assumptions in budgeting?
A: Budget exchange rate assumptions should be approved by finance leadership, with input from treasury, country teams, or program teams where the currency risk affects operations. This matters because the budget is not just an accounting estimate; it is often the basis for program planning, donor communication, and spending authority. If the rate assumption is unrealistic, the budget may look balanced on paper but fail in execution. Approval also creates accountability, so later variances can be traced back to the assumption set that was formally accepted. In multi-entity NGOs, the assumption may need to differ by country or by funding source, especially where spending and reporting currencies are not the same. That approval should be recorded in the budget assumptions log and referenced in the final budget pack.
Send to the team:
- “Please approve and document the FX assumptions before final budget sign-off.”
Key point:
- FX assumptions need formal approval.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
46. Q: How should an NGO budget for exchange rate movements?
A: It should use an approved base rate and then test one or more downside and upside scenarios so managers can see the effect of currency movement on the local-cost budget. That allows the organization to identify which lines are most sensitive, such as imports, contracted services, travel, or payroll support. A budget built this way is more realistic because it recognizes that the rate at approval time may not be the rate at execution time. It also gives leadership an early warning if the planned activity level becomes unaffordable under a weaker exchange rate. The most useful budget model shows the assumed rate, the stress rate, the resulting local-currency effect, and the operational response required if the currency weakens. That makes the budget a planning tool rather than just a numerical target.
Send to the team:
- “Please build FX sensitivity into the budget model and highlight the lines most exposed to currency movement.”
Key point:
- Budgeting should test multiple FX scenarios.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
47. Q: What is exchange rate volatility?
A: Exchange rate volatility is the size and frequency of changes in currency values over time. A currency that moves sharply in a short period creates more uncertainty for budgeting, cash planning, and settlement. Volatility is especially important for NGOs because program spending is often tied to fixed donor commitments, yet actual local-currency costs can change quickly. The more volatile the currency, the more important it becomes to review exposures, update assumptions, and explain variances clearly. Volatility also increases the chance that a balance will need a larger revaluation adjustment at month end. As a result, even simple cash management may need more frequent review when the organization operates in volatile currencies.
Send to the team:
- “Please watch for currencies that move sharply during the budget period and flag them in the forecast.”
Key point:
- Volatility makes financial planning less predictable.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
48. Q: How can exchange rate volatility affect programs?
A: It can reduce the local-currency value of funds, force activity cuts, delay purchases, or change how many beneficiaries can be served. A program that was planned with a certain cost per unit may suddenly cost more in local currency if the exchange rate moves against the organization. That creates pressure on implementation, because the finance issue becomes an operational issue very quickly. If the organization sees the risk early, it can adjust sequencing, procurement timing, or spending priorities before the impact becomes severe. Volatility can also affect sub-offices differently, especially when one office spends rapidly and another holds cash longer. That is why program teams and finance teams should review the impact together rather than separately.
Send to the team:
- “Please assess program impact whenever a major currency move affects budgeted activity.”
Key point:
- FX volatility can reduce program delivery capacity.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
49. Q: How can exchange rate volatility affect donor reporting?
A: It can create variances between budget and actual figures, change the local-currency value of restricted funds, and make comparisons across periods harder if the exchange movement is not explained. Donors generally want to see whether money was used for the approved purpose, but they also need to understand how much of the difference is due to currency movement rather than poor execution. If FX effects are buried, the report can look like the NGO underperformed when the real reason was rate movement. Clear donor reporting therefore needs a separate explanation of FX impact and a clean reconciliation to the underlying ledger. The report should also distinguish between the donor currency view and the functional-currency view so the donor can understand both the original commitment and the local operating effect. That makes the explanation more transparent and more defensible.
Send to the team:
- “Please explain FX effects separately in donor reports and show the donor-currency and functional-currency totals side by side.”
Key point:
- Donor reports should isolate FX impact.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
50. Q: How can exchange rate volatility affect cash planning?
A: It can make the functional-currency value of available cash rise or fall even when the foreign currency balance itself does not change. That means the NGO may appear to have enough cash in nominal foreign currency terms but still fall short in local-currency terms when it comes time to pay salaries, suppliers, or field costs. A reliable cash forecast should therefore include FX scenarios and not rely only on the raw foreign currency bank balance. When volatility is high, treasury and program teams need to review cash more frequently so the organization does not get caught with insufficient usable funds. Cash planning should also distinguish between immediately spendable funds and balances that are likely to be consumed later, because delayed use increases exposure. This makes the forecast more realistic and supports better treasury decisions.
Send to the team:
- “Please include FX scenarios in the cash forecast and update the plan when exchange rates move materially.”
Key point:
- Cash planning must reflect currency movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
51. Q: What is foreign currency translation for branches or entities?
A: Foreign currency translation is the process of converting a branch or entity’s financial statements from its functional currency into the reporting currency of the group or parent. It is not just a mechanical exchange-rate exercise; it is the step that lets one organization compare, combine, and present results from entities operating in different monetary environments. In a multi-country NGO, this is especially important because local records may be kept in one currency while leadership, donors, or the consolidating office need a different reporting currency for the whole organization. Translation also helps preserve comparability across periods, because the group can see local performance and group performance in one common reporting language without losing the original economic context.
Send to the team:
- “Please translate branch or entity results using the approved reporting currency rules and keep the workpapers clear enough for consolidation review.”
Key point:
- Translation converts whole financial statements for group reporting.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
52. Q: What is the difference between transaction accounting and translation accounting?
A: Transaction accounting records a foreign-currency transaction at the exchange rate on the transaction date and later remeasures or settles it when the balance remains open or is paid. Translation accounting is different because it converts an entire entity’s financial statements into another reporting currency after the entity has already prepared its books in its functional currency. The first is about one invoice, one bank payment, one receivable, or one payable; the second is about the full set of financial statements. In practice, this distinction matters because transaction accounting affects individual ledger items and foreign exchange gains or losses, while translation accounting affects group reporting and consolidation presentation. Confusing the two often leads to mispostings, double counting, or unexplained differences at close.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate foreign-currency transaction entries from reporting translation entries and label each workpaper clearly.”
Key point:
- Transactions affect individual items; translation affects entire statements.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
53. Q: When does translation apply in multi-entity NGOs?
A: Translation applies when a branch, subsidiary, field office, or project entity prepares accounts in a functional currency different from the group reporting currency. It becomes necessary at each reporting date because the group cannot combine local statements fairly until they are expressed in a common currency. This usually arises in NGOs with cross-border programs, regional hubs, or entities that operate locally but report centrally. Translation is therefore part of the regular close process, not an optional year-end adjustment. It ensures the group can read a complete picture of assets, liabilities, income, and expenses across all entities without changing the underlying local records.
Send to the team:
- “Please identify which entities need translation at each reporting date and keep the list updated as entity structures change.”
Key point:
- Translation is needed when reporting currencies differ.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
54. Q: What is the functional currency of an entity?
A: The functional currency is the main currency of the economic environment in which an entity operates and generates or spends cash. It is the base currency used for that entity’s accounting, meaning local transactions are first measured in the currency that most faithfully reflects the entity’s operating reality. For an NGO, this often follows the currency used for salaries, local procurement, bank accounts, and local grants, although the answer depends on the entity’s actual economic environment rather than just where it is incorporated. Getting this right matters because it drives transaction recording, foreign exchange measurement, and translation into any parent or group reporting currency. If the functional currency is wrong, the entire accounting layer can become distorted from the start.
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm the functional currency for each entity and branch and document the basis for the decision.”
Key point:
- Functional currency is the entity’s primary operating currency.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- GAO Green Book
- FASB ASC
55. Q: Why does functional currency matter in inter-entity accounting?
A: Functional currency matters because the same inter-entity balance may be recorded differently on each side depending on the currency in which each entity naturally keeps its books. One entity may owe money in its local currency while the other records the receivable or payable from its own reporting perspective and exchange-rate basis. That means the two sides can disagree even when the underlying business event is the same. In a multi-entity NGO, this can create recurring reconciliation items unless the group has a disciplined method for recording, remeasuring, and closing internal balances. Functional currency therefore affects not only the local ledger but also the timing and shape of consolidation adjustments.
Send to the team:
- “Please code inter-entity balances with the correct functional currency on both sides and document any rate differences.”
Key point:
- Currency choice affects how each side records the balance.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
56. Q: What is an inter-entity balance?
A: An inter-entity balance is an amount owed between two entities within the same NGO group, such as a due to / due from account. It records internal claims and obligations rather than external liabilities or receivables. These balances often arise when one office pays on behalf of another, when headquarters advances cash to a field entity, or when one legal entity recharges another for shared costs. In the books, the balance should be obvious as internal, because its purpose is to track movement within the group until the amount is settled or eliminated. In consolidation, these balances must be identified quickly so they do not remain in the group’s external-looking financial statements.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep internal balances in separate inter-entity accounts and do not mix them with third-party balances.”
Key point:
- Inter-entity balances are internal payables and receivables.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
- FASB ASC
57. Q: Why do inter-entity balances arise in NGOs?
A: They arise when one entity pays for costs on behalf of another, sends cash internally, charges shared costs, or records transfers before the other entity does. They are common in multi-office and multi-country NGOs because internal support, cash pooling, and shared services often require one legal entity or branch to carry costs temporarily. A headquarters office may pay a vendor invoice for a country office, then recharge the amount later. A regional office may transfer funds to a field office before the field office recognizes the related expense. All of these create internal balances that need tracking, matching, and clearing so the group can manage resources transparently.
Send to the team:
- “Please log every internal settlement or cross-charge at the time it happens so the matching entry is not forgotten.”
Key point:
- Internal activity creates internal balances.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
58. Q: How should inter-entity balances be recorded?
A: They should be recorded in matching due to / due from accounts with clear entity names, dates, amounts, currency, and purpose. Both sides should use consistent coding and supporting documents so that anyone reviewing the ledger can understand why the balance exists and what should clear it. Good recording practice also includes a reference to the source transaction, such as a cash transfer, cross-charge, or shared procurement item. If the balance is not well described, it becomes difficult to reconcile later, especially when multiple internal items accumulate in the same month. Recording discipline at the start reduces month-end confusion and makes consolidation much more reliable.
Send to the team:
- “Please record all inter-entity items with matching counterpart details and keep the support attached.”
Key point:
- Matching detail makes internal balances traceable.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
59. Q: Why must inter-entity balances be reconciled regularly?
A: Regular reconciliation confirms that both entities agree on the same amount and same nature of the balance. It prevents errors from carrying into consolidation and month-end reporting, where a small mismatch can become a bigger reporting problem if left unresolved. Reconciliation also helps identify missing journals, timing delays, incorrect exchange rates, and coding mistakes before they affect management reporting or donor reporting. In a well-run NGO, inter-entity reconciliation is not a year-end clean-up task; it is a routine control that happens every reporting cycle. The discipline of matching both sides early is what keeps internal accounts from drifting apart over time.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile inter-entity balances every reporting cycle and escalate differences that remain open after review.”
Key point:
- Reconciliation prevents internal mismatches from growing.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
60. Q: What causes differences in inter-entity balances?
A: Common causes include timing differences, different exchange rates, missing entries, miscodes, unposted journals, and unsettled cross-charges. Differences may also come from cut-off errors, where one entity has already posted a transaction while the other has not yet recorded the corresponding side. In foreign-currency groups, the same internal item may also look different because each entity used a different rate or a different posting date. Some differences are harmless and temporary, but others signal poor process discipline or unsupported accounting. The key is to separate true timing gaps from real errors and resolve the latter quickly.
Send to the team:
- “Please investigate timing, coding, and currency differences first before treating any item as unresolved.”
Key point:
- Most mismatches come from timing or coding.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS IAS 21
61. Q: What is an intercompany or inter-entity elimination?
A: An elimination is a consolidation entry that removes internal balances, transactions, income, or expenses between group entities so the consolidated statements show only external activity. It is used because the group should not present internal trading, internal receivables, or internal payables as if they were outside the organization. Without elimination, the same value can appear twice in the consolidated statements, once from each side of the internal transaction. Elimination is therefore the step that turns separate-entity books into true group-level reporting. It is one of the most important adjustments in consolidation because it preserves the integrity of the final statements.
Send to the team:
- “Please eliminate internal balances and internal revenue in consolidation using a documented workpaper.”
Key point:
- Elimination prevents double counting inside the group.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
62. Q: Why are elimination entries needed in consolidation?
A: Elimination entries are needed because internal transfers do not represent external income, external expense, or external assets for the group as a whole. If headquarters sends money to a country office, the group has not become richer or poorer simply because the cash moved within itself. The same logic applies to internal charges, internal reimbursements, and internal balances. Consolidated reports are intended to show the NGO as one economic unit, so internal activity must disappear from the final view. This is why elimination entries are more than a technical step; they are a statement of reporting truth.
Send to the team:
- “Please remove internal activity before final group reporting and keep the elimination schedule tied to the source balances.”
Key point:
- Consolidation should show the group as one economic unit.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
63. Q: What is the difference between a transfer and a settlement between entities?
A: A transfer is movement of value inside the NGO group. A settlement is the clearing of the related balance between the entities, usually through cash, journal entry, or netting. The transfer creates the internal receivable or payable, while the settlement removes or reduces it. This distinction matters because a transfer may happen on one date while the settlement occurs later, and the books should show both stages clearly. If the settlement is not recorded, the balance may remain open long after the practical issue has been resolved.
Send to the team:
- “Please distinguish internal transfers from actual settlement of the balance and post both stages correctly.”
Key point:
- Transfer creates the balance; settlement clears it.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
64. Q: How should shared transactions between entities be documented?
A: Shared transactions should have a source document, the entity paying or receiving the cost, the allocation basis, the currency, the date, and approval evidence. The documentation should make clear why the split was made and how the amount was calculated. If the item relates to staff time, office rent, utilities, or regional services, the workpaper should show the driver and the proportion applied. This makes the charge defensible if reviewed by management, auditors, or another entity in the group. Good documentation also makes future rechecks much faster because the reasoning is visible instead of buried in email history.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep support for the allocation basis on every shared transaction and file it with the journal.”
Key point:
- Shared cost documentation should show the logic of the split.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
65. Q: What is a recharge between entities?
A: A recharge is an internal billing where one entity passes a cost or portion of a cost to another entity. It is common when one office pays a vendor directly but the benefit belongs partly or entirely to another entity in the group. The recharge should usually create a receivable in the billing entity and a payable in the charged entity until the amount is settled. It should also be supported by an agreed basis, such as headcount, square footage, direct benefit, or time spent. When done correctly, recharges help the group assign costs fairly without losing control of the underlying cash and accounting flows.
Send to the team:
- “Please use approved recharge rules and issue the internal billing promptly.”
Key point:
- A recharge reallocates a cost inside the group.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
66. Q: How should cross-charges between entities be handled?
A: Cross-charges should be based on documented agreements, approved allocation keys, and matching entries in both entities. They should be reviewed for fairness, timing, and currency consistency so that one side does not recognize a cost or revenue that the other side never intended to record. In practice, cross-charges can cover shared staff, IT services, procurement support, admin overhead, or regional management costs. The handling should be routine, transparent, and repeatable rather than negotiated afresh each month. If the cross-charge basis changes, the change should be documented so reporting users can understand the movement.
Send to the team:
- “Please support every cross-charge with a clear allocation method and matching journal entries on both sides.”
Key point:
- Cross-charges need a documented and consistent basis.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
67. Q: What is the risk of not reconciling inter-entity accounts?
A: The risk is that errors, mispostings, missing settlements, and currency differences remain hidden and distort consolidation. It can also mask fraud, unsupported balances, or simple process breakdowns because the same item may sit open on both sides for months. When inter-entity accounts are not reconciled, the group may overstate assets, liabilities, revenue, or expenses and produce reports that look complete but are not actually reliable. The problem tends to grow slowly, then becomes difficult to unwind at year-end. Regular reconciliation is therefore one of the most effective controls for preventing small internal issues from becoming major reporting problems.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile every inter-entity account before close and track unresolved items to completion.”
Key point:
- Unreconciled balances can distort group results and hide issues.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
68. Q: How should due to and due from accounts be monitored?
A: They should be monitored with an aging review, monthly reconciliation, owner assignment, and escalation for old items. Large or stale items should be explained and resolved quickly so the balances do not become permanent placeholders for unfinished work. Monitoring should also include a review of any unusual currency differences, repeated manual adjustments, or balances that move in one direction without settlement. A good monitoring process makes it obvious which items are current, which are timing-related, and which are overdue. That distinction helps management focus on the balances that actually need intervention.
Send to the team:
- “Please review aging and unresolved items every month and assign follow-up owners.”
Key point:
- Due to / due from balances need active follow-up.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
69. Q: What is the role of inter-entity confirmations?
A: Inter-entity confirmations provide written evidence that both sides agree on the balance, currency, and nature of the transaction. They strengthen the reconciliation process by turning an internal assumption into a documented agreement between the two entities. This is especially valuable where amounts are material, where several entities interact, or where internal support spans multiple countries and currencies. Confirmations can also reveal missing entries earlier, because one side may recognize a balance that the other side has not yet recorded. In short, they help move the process from “we think it matches” to “we have evidence it matches.”
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm inter-entity balances in writing at close for all material items.”
Key point:
- Confirmations create evidence of agreement.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
70. Q: Who should own inter-entity reconciliation?
A: Ownership should sit with finance at the entity level, usually the accountant or controller responsible for the balances, with oversight by group finance. Clear ownership prevents items from being ignored because everyone assumes someone else will clear them. The local owner is usually closest to the source document and can explain why the amount exists, while the group controller can challenge unusual differences and enforce close deadlines. This layered ownership model works well in NGOs because it balances local knowledge with central oversight. Without ownership, inter-entity accounts become orphan balances that survive from one period to the next.
Send to the team:
- “Please assign a named owner for each inter-entity account and include it in the close checklist.”
Key point:
- Every internal balance needs a clear owner.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
71. Q: What is an inter-entity reconciliation template?
A: It is a working format used to compare both entities’ records for the same internal balance. It usually shows opening balance, additions, settlements, FX movements, and closing balance, along with supporting notes for items still under review. A strong template allows finance staff to see where differences arise without rebuilding the calculation from scratch every month. It also creates consistency, which is important when different offices or teams prepare the reconciliation over time. The template becomes a control tool, a management tool, and an audit support schedule all at once.
Send to the team:
- “Please use one standard template for every inter-entity account and keep the same logic each period.”
Key point:
- A template standardizes the review of internal balances.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
72. Q: What information should appear in an inter-entity reconciliation?
A: It should show both entity names, account codes, currency, opening balance, transactions, settlements, exchange differences, timing items, and the final agreed balance. Support should be attached for unresolved items so the reviewer can see why the remaining difference still exists. Good reconciliations also include preparer and reviewer sign-off, because ownership matters as much as arithmetic. If the account spans several months, the schedule should preserve the prior period history to show whether the issue is recurring or newly created. The aim is to make the reconciliation readable, traceable, and ready for close review without extra explanation by email.
Send to the team:
- “Please include dates, codes, currency, and explanations in the reconciliation and attach supporting evidence for exceptions.”
Key point:
- Good reconciliations show both amounts and reasons.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
73. Q: How should timing differences be resolved between entities?
A: Timing differences should be explained, matched to the next posting cycle, and cleared through follow-up journals or settlements. Items that remain open should be escalated and tracked rather than left as “temporary” indefinitely. The best practice is to log the difference, note which entity posted first, identify when the counter-entry is expected, and then confirm closure in the next cycle. This creates a disciplined trail and reduces the risk that an old timing item turns into a permanent unexplained balance. In a monthly close, timely follow-up is often more important than debating the accounting theory of the item.
Send to the team:
- “Please log timing differences and clear them in the next close cycle.”
Key point:
- Timing differences should be temporary and monitored.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
74. Q: What is the role of cut-off in foreign currency and inter-entity accounting?
A: Cut-off determines which period records the transaction and at what exchange rate or balance date it belongs. It is critical because late postings can create FX differences, reconciliation breaks, and mismatches between the entities’ books. If a transfer is booked after month-end in one office but before month-end in another, the internal balances will not align even if the underlying transaction is real. The same issue arises when exchange rates are locked on different dates or at different times. Strong cut-off discipline keeps the accounting period boundary clear and prevents avoidable close noise.
Send to the team:
- “Please apply cut-off consistently at month-end and year-end and document any late postings.”
Key point:
- Cut-off errors create both timing and FX issues.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- GAO Green Book
75. Q: What happens when one entity records a transaction before another?
A: A temporary mismatch appears until the other entity posts the matching entry. If not monitored, the difference may look like an error even when it is only timing, but if the mismatch stays open too long it can become a real control issue. Good finance teams track one-sided postings, ask who should have the counter-entry, and set a follow-up date for confirmation. This is common in intercompany cash transfers, shared costs, and cross-border recharges. The point is not to force both entities to post on the exact same minute, but to ensure the gap is understood and closed within the reporting cycle.
Send to the team:
- “Please track unmatched items until both sides post and keep the list visible during close.”
Key point:
- One-sided posting usually creates a timing gap.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
76. Q: What is a mismatch in inter-entity coding?
A: A mismatch in inter-entity coding happens when the two entities use different account codes, entity codes, or descriptions for the same internal transaction. This makes matching, review, and elimination harder because the items do not line up cleanly in the consolidation file or the reconciliation schedule. Coding mismatches often start small, especially when local teams use their own naming conventions or copy prior postings without checking the current structure. Over time, the mismatch can create a trail of items that are technically the same but operationally difficult to match. Standard coding rules and mapping tables are the simplest protection against this problem.
Send to the team:
- “Please use matched entity and account codes on both sides and check the mapping before posting.”
Key point:
- Coding mismatches block clean reconciliation.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS 10
77. Q: How should different chart of accounts structures be aligned across entities?
A: The group should maintain a mapping table that links local codes to group codes and standard reporting categories. This allows local autonomy without losing consolidation consistency, because each entity can keep the detail it needs while still feeding the group report in a common format. The mapping should be maintained centrally, reviewed when new accounts are created, and updated when reporting lines change. If each entity invents its own chart structure without a mapping, group reporting becomes manual and error-prone. A controlled mapping approach preserves flexibility but still gives the group a reliable reporting backbone.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a chart-of-accounts mapping for every entity and review it during close.”
Key point:
- Mapping makes different local systems comparable.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
78. Q: What is the impact of foreign currency on consolidation?
A: Foreign currency can change reported balances, translated results, and equity movements when entity statements are converted into the group currency. It can also create translation differences that must be tracked separately from normal operating surpluses or deficits. This means the group’s totals may move even if no new activity occurred in local operations, simply because exchange rates changed between reporting dates. For leadership and donors, that distinction is essential because it explains why reported performance can differ from operational reality. Consolidation therefore has to isolate FX effects so users can understand what is business-driven and what is currency-driven.
Send to the team:
- “Please translate foreign-currency entities using the approved closing and average rates and explain the translation movement clearly.”
Key point:
- FX affects the group’s reported totals even without new business activity.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
79. Q: What is the impact of inter-entity balances on consolidation?
A: Inter-entity balances do not belong in the consolidated statement of financial position, so they must be eliminated. If not removed, assets and liabilities are overstated because the group would be showing claims against itself as though they were external balances. The same issue can arise with internal revenue and internal expenses, which would inflate the group’s activity if left in place. Consolidation is meant to show the group as a single reporting unit, so internal balances have to disappear from the final presentation. This is why reconciliation and elimination need to be linked tightly in the close process.
Send to the team:
- “Please eliminate all confirmed internal balances before consolidation and verify that the source schedules agree.”
Key point:
- Internal balances must disappear in group reporting.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
80. Q: What is a consolidation adjustment?
A: A consolidation adjustment is an entry made at group level to restate or eliminate items so the consolidated financial statements are presented correctly. It may include eliminations, reclassifications, or translation adjustments depending on the structure of the group and the nature of the underlying transaction. Unlike a routine journal in a local entity, a consolidation adjustment is designed to produce a group view rather than to change the local ledger. The adjustment should be supported by a workpaper that shows the source balances, the logic applied, and the final consolidated result. Good consolidation adjustments are traceable, limited to the needed corrections, and reviewed by someone who understands the full group structure.
Send to the team:
- “Please support every consolidation adjustment with a clear workpaper and reviewer sign-off.”
Key point:
- Consolidation adjustments reshape local numbers into group numbers.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- IFRS IAS 21
81. Q: Why must inter-entity balances be eliminated on consolidation?
A: They must be eliminated because the group cannot owe itself money in the consolidated statements. Keeping them would double count both assets and liabilities and would mislead users about the scale of external obligations and external resources. The same logic applies to internal income and expenditure, which are only movement inside the group, not activity with outside parties. Elimination is therefore not an optional cleanup step; it is a core principle of presenting the group fairly. When done well, the consolidated statements show only the organization’s position relative to outsiders, not the back-and-forth inside the structure.
Send to the team:
- “Please remove all internal due to / due from balances in the consolidation file and confirm the eliminations against the reconciliations.”
Key point:
- A consolidated group should not show internal claims against itself.
Official links:
- IFRS 10
- GAO Green Book
82. Q: How should foreign currency translation differences appear in reporting?
A: Translation differences should be shown separately from operating results so users can distinguish business performance from currency movement. They are not the same as program overspending, revenue shortfall, or cost inflation, even though they may change the bottom line in the reporting currency. Separating them improves clarity for management, donors, and boards because it shows whether the movement came from local operations or from exchange-rate movement. Where the reporting framework allows, these differences are usually tracked in equity or a reserve line rather than being mixed into ordinary activity. That presentation helps preserve the meaning of the operating result.
Send to the team:
- “Please present translation differences separately from operating surplus or deficit and explain any material movement in the notes.”
Key point:
- Translation effects should not be mixed with operating results.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
83. Q: What is a currency revaluation reserve?
A: A currency revaluation reserve is the equity account used to accumulate certain translation differences from foreign operations under the reporting framework. It helps keep unrealized translation effects separate from current-period operating results so that the group’s performance report does not become cluttered with currency-only movement. The reserve acts as a memory of currency translation effects until they are released or otherwise treated under the applicable reporting rules. For an NGO with multiple foreign entities, this reserve can become an important part of explaining the difference between local operating results and group reporting outcomes. It also gives management a clearer view of how much of the movement is structural versus currency-driven.
Send to the team:
- “Please track translation movements in the correct reserve account and reconcile it during consolidation.”
Key point:
- The reserve separates currency effects from operations.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- IFRS 10
84. Q: How should FX differences be disclosed in reports?
A: FX differences should be described clearly in the notes or commentary so users can see the source of the movement, the rate used, and whether the difference is realized or unrealized. Material amounts should be explained in plain language, especially when they affect donor reports or management commentary. Good disclosure separates operational performance from rate effects and makes it obvious whether a movement came from cash settlement, remeasurement, or translation. The report should also identify any unusually large rate swings or assumptions used in the calculation. Clear disclosure protects credibility because it shows the organization understands why the numbers moved, not just that they moved.
Send to the team:
- “Please include FX explanations in the monthly and donor report commentary whenever the movement is material.”
Key point:
- Disclosure should explain the source and effect of FX movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21
- GAO Green Book
85. Q: What controls reduce exchange rate error risk?
A: Controls include approved rate sources, rate review, locked rate tables, independent checks, and documented cut-off dates. Using one standard source reduces inconsistent entries across branches and prevents teams from choosing different rates for similar transactions. A good control environment also defines who updates the rate, when the update happens, and how the rate is applied to settlements, open balances, and translations. If the group works across several countries, the rate policy should make it clear whether rates are daily, monthly average, closing, or transaction-specific. Strong rate controls reduce both accounting errors and the time spent investigating them later.
Send to the team:
- “Please use one approved exchange-rate source and review it before posting.”
Key point:
- Approved rates prevent avoidable FX errors.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IFRS IAS 21
86. Q: What controls reduce inter-entity reconciliation risk?
A: Controls include monthly matching, named ownership, confirmation, aging review, standard templates, and escalation of old items.
